  {{Distinguish|Obsessive–compulsive personality disorder}}
}}

{{Use American English|date=December 2017}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2025}}
{{Infobox medical condition
| name = Obsessive–compulsive disorder
| image = OCD Cycle.svg
| caption = The four components of the OCD cycle
| field = [[Psychiatry]], [[clinical psychology]]
| symptoms = Feel the need to perform [[compulsive behavior|certain behaviors repeatedly]], have [[intrusive thought|certain distressing thoughts repeatedly]]<ref name="NIH20152" />
| complications = [[tic disorders|Tics]], [[anxiety disorder]], [[suicide]]<ref name="DSM52" /><ref name="Ang2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Angelakis I, Gooding P, Tarrier N, Panagioti M | title = Suicidality in obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD): a systematic review and meta-analysis | journal = Clinical Psychology Review | volume = 39 | pages = 1–15 | date = July 2015 | pmid = 25875222 | doi = 10.1016/j.cpr.2015.03.002 | publisher = [[Pergamon Press]] }}</ref>
| onset = Before 35 years<ref name="NIH20152" /><ref name="DSM52" />
| risks = [[Genetics]], [[neurobiology]], [[temperament]], [[Child abuse|childhood trauma]], certain [[drugs]] and [[medication]]s<ref name="NIH20152" />
| diagnosis = Clinically based on symptoms; Y-BOCS is the gold standard tool to assess severity<ref name="DSM52" />
| differential = [[Anxiety disorder]], [[major depressive disorder]], [[eating disorder]]s, [[tic disorder]]s, [[obsessive–compulsive personality disorder]]<ref name="DSM52" />
| treatment = [[Cognitive behavioral therapy]], [[medication]], [[transcranial magnetic stimulation]], [[surgery]]<ref name="NEJM2014" /><ref name="Ve2014" />
| medication = [[Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor]]s (SSRIs), [[clomipramine]], adjunctive [[atypical antipsychotics]]
| frequency = 2.3%<ref name="Good2014" />
}}

<!-- Definition and symptoms -->
'''Obsessive–compulsive disorder''' ('''OCD''') is a [[mental disorder]] in which an individual has [[intrusive thought]]s (an ''obsession'') and feels the need to perform certain behaviors (''[[Compulsive behavior|compulsions]]'') repeatedly to relieve the distress caused by the obsession, to the extent where it impairs general function.<ref name="NIH20152">{{Cite web |last=The National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) |date=January 2016 |title=What is Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD)? |url=https://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/topics/obsessive-compulsive-disorder-ocd |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160723194024/http://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/topics/obsessive-compulsive-disorder-ocd/index.shtml |archive-date=23 July 2016 |access-date=24 July 2016 |website=[[U.S. National Institutes of Health]] (NIH)}}</ref><ref name="DSM52">{{Cite book |isbn=978-0-89042-555-8 |url=https://archive.org/details/diagnosticstatis0005unse |title=Diagnostic and statistical manual of mental disorders: DSM-5 |date=2013 |edition=5th |location=Washington |pages=[https://archive.org/details/diagnosticstatis0005unse/page/237 237–242] |publisher=American Psychiatric Publishing}}</ref><ref name="Sartorius">{{Cite web | vauthors =  Sartorius N, Henderson A, Strotzka H, Lipowski Z, Yu-cun S, You-xin X, Strömgren E, Glatzel J, Kühne G, Misès R, Soldatos C, Pull C, Giel R, Jegede R, Malt U, Nadzharov R, Smulevitch A, Hagberg B, Perris C, Scharfetter C, Clare A, Cooper J, Corbett J, Griffith Edwards J, Gelder M, Goldberg D, Gossop M, Graham P, Kendell R, Marks I, Russell G, Rutter M, Shepherd M, West D, Wing J, Wing L, Neki J, Benson F, Cantwell D, Guze S, Helzer J, Holzman P, Kleinman A, Kupfer D, Mezzich J, Spitzer R, Lokar J |title=The ICD-10 Classification of Mental and Behavioural Disorders Clinical descriptions and diagnostic guidelines |website=who.int [[World Health Organization]] |page=142 |url=https://apps.who.int/iris/bitstream/handle/10665/37958/9241544228_eng.pdf |access-date=8 August 2023}}</ref> OCD has been described since antiquity and has affected numerous notable historical and contemporary figures; understandings of it were historically rooted in religion and beliefs about [[demonic possession]].

Obsessions are persistent unwanted thoughts, mental images, or urges that generate feelings of [[anxiety]], [[disgust]], or [[discomfort]].<ref name="nhs.uk_2021a">{{Cite web |date=16 February 2021 |title=Overview – Obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD) |url=https://www.nhs.uk/mental-health/conditions/obsessive-compulsive-disorder-ocd/overview/ |access-date=6 November 2021 |website=[[National Health Service|nhs]].uk |language=en}}</ref> Some common obsessions include fear of [[contamination]], obsession with [[symmetry]], the fear of acting [[Blasphemy|blasphemously]], [[sexual obsessions]], and the fear of possibly harming others or themselves.<ref name="NIH20152" /><ref name="psychiatry.org">{{Cite web |title=What Is Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder? |url=https://www.psychiatry.org/patients-families/ocd/what-is-obsessive-compulsive-disorder |access-date=6 November 2021 |website=[[psychiatry.org]]}}</ref> Compulsions are repetitive actions performed in response to obsessions to reduce anxiety, such as washing, checking, counting, reassurance seeking, and situational avoidance.<ref name="NIH20152" /><ref name="psychiatry.org" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=CDC |date=2 December 2020 |title=Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder in Children {{!}} CDC |url=https://www.cdc.gov/childrensmentalhealth/ocd.html |access-date=6 November 2021 |website=[[Centers for Disease Control and Prevention]] |language=en-us}}</ref>

Compulsions occur often and typically take up at least one hour per day, impairing one's quality of life.<ref name="NIH20152" /><ref name="psychiatry.org" /> Compulsions temporarily relieve distress but reinforce obsessions over time. Many adults with OCD recognize their rituals as irrational yet continue them to reduce anxiety.<ref name="NIH20152" /><ref name="nhs.uk_2021a" /><ref name="psychiatry.org" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=What are compulsions? {{!}} OCD-UK |url=https://www.ocduk.org/ocd/compulsions/ |access-date=5 November 2021 |language=en-GB}}</ref> For this reason, thoughts and behaviors in OCD are usually considered [[egodystonic]] (inconsistent with one's ideal self-image).

<!-- Cause and diagnosis -->The causes of OCD are [[Multifactorial disease|multifactorial]] and not fully understood, involving [[genetic predisposition]], environmental stressors such as [[childhood trauma]], abnormalities in brain structure and [[neurotransmitter]] function, certain medications or drugs, potential autoimmune processes in some children, and possibly evolutionary factors influencing compulsive behaviors.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Browne HA, Gair SL, Scharf JM, Grice DE | title = Genetics of obsessive-compulsive disorder and related disorders | journal = The Psychiatric Clinics of North America | volume = 37 | issue = 3 | pages = 319–335 | date = September 2014 | pmid = 25150565 | pmc = 4143777 | doi = 10.1016/j.psc.2014.06.002 }}</ref><ref name="NIH20152" /><ref name="Symptoms of Obsessive-Compulsive Di">{{cite journal | vauthors = Starcevic V, Eslick GD, Viswasam K, Berle D | title = Symptoms of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder during Pregnancy and the Postpartum Period: a Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis | journal = The Psychiatric Quarterly | volume = 91 | issue = 4 | pages = 965–981 | date = December 2020 | pmid = 32445002 | doi = 10.1007/s11126-020-09769-8 }}</ref> Diagnosis is based on clinical presentation; rating scales such as the [[Yale–Brown Obsessive–Compulsive Scale]] (Y-BOCS) assess severity.<ref name="DSM52" /><ref name="Fenske_2009">{{cite journal | vauthors = Fenske JN, Schwenk TL | title = Obsessive compulsive disorder: diagnosis and management | journal = American Family Physician | volume = 80 | issue = 3 | pages = 239–245 | date = August 2009 | pmid = 19621834 | url = http://www.aafp.org/afp/2009/0801/p239.html | url-status = live | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20140512222223/http://www.aafp.org/afp/2009/0801/p239.html | archive-date = 12 May 2014 }}</ref> OCD involves abnormalities in cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuits and dysregulation of [[serotonin]], [[dopamine]], and [[Glutamic acid|glutamate]]. OCD is associated with a general increase in [[Suicide|suicidality]].<ref name="Ang2015" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Alonso P, Segalàs C, Real E, Pertusa A, Labad J, Jiménez-Murcia S, Jaurrieta N, Bueno B, Vallejo J, Menchón JM | title = Suicide in patients treated for obsessive-compulsive disorder: a prospective follow-up study | journal = Journal of Affective Disorders | volume = 124 | issue = 3 | pages = 300–308 | date = August 2010 | pmid = 20060171 | doi = 10.1016/j.jad.2009.12.001 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Albert U, De Ronchi D, Maina G, Pompili M | title = Suicide Risk in Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder and Exploration of Risk Factors: A Systematic Review | journal = Current Neuropharmacology | volume = 17 | issue = 8 | pages = 681–696 | date = 25 July 2019 | pmid = 29929465 | pmc = 7059158 | doi = 10.2174/1570159X16666180620155941 }}</ref> The term ''obsessive–compulsive'' or ''OCD'' is often used informally to describe someone overly meticulous or fixated, but OCD can present in many ways, and not all sufferers focus on cleanliness or symmetry.<ref name="Bynum_1985">{{Cite book | vauthors = Bynum WF, Porter R, Shepherd M |title=The anatomy of madness: essays in the history of psychiatry |date=1985 |publisher=[[Routledge]] |isbn=978-0-415-32382-6 |location=London |pages=166–187 |chapter=Obsessional Disorders: A Conceptual History. Terminological and Classificatory Issues.}}</ref><!-- Treatment and prognosis -->

OCD is chronic and long-lasting with periods of severe symptoms followed by periods of improvement.<ref name="NIMH" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=Obsessive-compulsive disorder: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia |url=https://medlineplus.gov/ency/article/000929.htm |access-date=25 April 2023 |website=medlineplus.gov |language=en}}</ref> Treatment can improve ability to function and quality of life, and is usually reflected by improved Y-BOCS scores.<ref>{{Cite web |date=October 2022 |title=What Is Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder? |url=https://www.psychiatry.org/patients-families/obsessive-compulsive-disorder/what-is-obsessive-compulsive-disorder |access-date=25 April 2023 |website=American Psychiatric Association}}</ref> First-line treatment for OCD typically consists of either [[exposure and response prevention]] or [[pharmacotherapy]] with [[selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors]] (SSRIs), or both in combination.<ref name="NEJM2014">{{cite journal | vauthors = Grant JE | title = Clinical practice: Obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = The New England Journal of Medicine | volume = 371 | issue = 7 | pages = 646–653 | date = August 2014 | pmid = 25119610 | doi = 10.1056/NEJMcp1402176 }}</ref><ref name="Ve2014" /><ref name="Beyond_OCD">{{Cite web |title=Medications Approved for Treatment of OCD |url=https://beyondocd.org/ocd-facts/approved-medications |access-date=11 December 2021 |website=Beyond OCD: OCD Information and Resources}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pittenger C, Bloch MH | title = Pharmacological treatment of obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = The Psychiatric Clinics of North America | volume = 37 | issue = 3 | pages = 375–391 | date = September 2014 | pmid = 25150568 | pmc = 4143776 | doi = 10.1016/j.psc.2014.05.006 }}</ref> Some patients fail to improve after treatment with SSRIs alone; these cases qualify as treatment-resistant and can require second-line treatment such as [[clomipramine]] or augmentation with an [[atypical antipsychotic]].<ref name="NEJM2014" /><ref name="Ve2014">{{cite journal | vauthors = Veale D, Miles S, Smallcombe N, Ghezai H, Goldacre B, Hodsoll J | title = Atypical antipsychotic augmentation in SSRI treatment refractory obsessive-compulsive disorder: a systematic review and meta-analysis | journal = BMC Psychiatry | volume = 14 | article-number = 317 | date = November 2014 | pmid = 25432131 | pmc = 4262998 | doi = 10.1186/s12888-014-0317-5 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name="Bloch_2010">{{cite journal | vauthors = Bloch MH, McGuire J, Landeros-Weisenberger A, Leckman JF, Pittenger C | title = Meta-analysis of the dose-response relationship of SSRI in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Molecular Psychiatry | volume = 15 | issue = 8 | pages = 850–855 | date = August 2010 | pmid = 19468281 | pmc = 2888928 | doi = 10.1038/mp.2009.50 }}</ref><ref name="Dec2010">{{cite journal | vauthors = Decloedt EH, Stein DJ | title = Current trends in drug treatment of obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Neuropsychiatric Disease and Treatment | volume = 6 | pages = 233–242 | date = May 2010 | pmid = 20520787 | pmc = 2877605 | doi = 10.2147/NDT.S3149 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Treatment-resistant obsessive–compulsive disorder is also managed with [[transcranial magnetic stimulation]] or, as a last resort, surgical options like [[deep brain stimulation]].<ref name="Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: Adva">{{cite journal | vauthors = Hirschtritt ME, Bloch MH, Mathews CA | title = Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: Advances in Diagnosis and Treatment | journal = JAMA | volume = 317 | issue = 13 | pages = 1358–1367 | date = April 2017 | pmid = 28384832 | doi = 10.1001/jama.2017.2200 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Gadot R, Najera R, Hirani S, Anand A, Storch E, Goodman WK, Shofty B, Sheth SA | title = Efficacy of deep brain stimulation for treatment-resistant obsessive-compulsive disorder: systematic review and meta-analysis | journal = Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery, and Psychiatry | volume = 93 | issue = 11 | pages = 1166–1173 | date = September 2022 | pmid = 36127157 | doi = 10.1136/jnnp-2021-328738 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name=":0" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fitzsimmons SM, van der Werf YD, van Campen AD, Arns M, Sack AT, Hoogendoorn AW, van den Heuvel OA | title = Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation for obsessive-compulsive disorder: A systematic review and pairwise/network meta-analysis | journal = Journal of Affective Disorders | volume = 302 | pages = 302–312 | date = April 2022 | pmid = 35041869 | doi = 10.1016/j.jad.2022.01.048 | hdl-access = free | doi-access = free | hdl = 2066/247522 }}</ref>

{{TOC limit}}

== Signs and symptoms ==
OCD can present with a wide variety of symptoms. Certain groups of symptoms usually occur together as dimensions or clusters, which may reflect an underlying process. The standard assessment tool for OCD, the [[Yale–Brown Obsessive–Compulsive Scale]] (Y-BOCS), has 13 predefined categories of symptoms. These symptoms fit into three to five groupings.<ref name="Leckman">{{cite journal | vauthors = Leckman JF, Bloch MH, King RA | title = Symptom dimensions and subtypes of obsessive-compulsive disorder: a developmental perspective | journal = Dialogues in Clinical Neuroscience | volume = 11 | issue = 1 | pages = 21–33 | date = 2009 | pmid = 19432385 | pmc = 3181902 | doi = 10.31887/DCNS.2009.11.1/jfleckman }}</ref> A [[meta-analysis|meta-analytic]] review of symptom structures found a four-factor grouping structure to be most reliable: symmetry factor, forbidden thoughts factor, cleaning factor and hoarding factor. The symmetry factor correlates highly with obsessions related to ordering, counting and symmetry, as well as repeating compulsions. The forbidden thoughts factor correlates highly with intrusive thoughts of a violent, religious, or sexual nature. The cleaning factor correlates highly with obsessions about contamination and compulsions related to cleaning. The hoarding factor only involves hoarding-related obsessions and compulsions, and was identified as being distinct from other symptom groupings.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bloch MH, Landeros-Weisenberger A, Rosario MC, Pittenger C, Leckman JF | title = Meta-analysis of the symptom structure of obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = [[The American Journal of Psychiatry]] | volume = 165 | issue = 12 | pages = 1532–1542 | date = December 2008 | pmid = 18923068 | pmc = 3972003 | doi = 10.1176/appi.ajp.2008.08020320 }}</ref>

When examining the onset of OCD, one study suggests that there are differences in the age of onset between males and females, with the average age of onset of OCD being 9.6 years for boys and 11.0 years for girls.<ref name="Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder in Ch">{{cite journal | vauthors = Swedo SE, Rapoport JL, Leonard H, Lenane M, Cheslow D | title = Obsessive-compulsive disorder in children and adolescents. Clinical phenomenology of 70 consecutive cases | journal = Archives of General Psychiatry | volume = 46 | issue = 4 | pages = 335–341 | date = April 1989 | pmid = 2930330 | doi = 10.1001/archpsyc.1989.01810040041007 }}</ref> Children with OCD often have other mental disorders, such as ADHD, [[Depression (mood)|depression]], anxiety, and disruptive behavior disorder. Continually, children are more likely to struggle in school and experience difficulties in social situations.<ref name="m564">{{cite journal | last=Lack | first=Caleb W | title=Obsessive-compulsive disorder: Evidence-based treatments and future directions for research | journal=World Journal of Psychiatry | volume=2 | issue=6 | date=2012 | pages=86–90 | pmid=24175173 | pmc=3782190 | doi=10.5498/wjp.v2.i6.86 | doi-access=free }}</ref> When looking at both adults and children, a study found the average ages of onset to be 21 and 24 for males and females respectively.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Rasmussen SA, Eisen JL | title = The epidemiology and clinical features of obsessive compulsive disorder | journal = The Psychiatric Clinics of North America | volume = 15 | issue = 4 | pages = 743–758 | date = December 1992 | pmid = 1461792 | doi = 10.1016/S0193-953X(18)30205-3 }}</ref> While some studies have shown that OCD with earlier onset is associated with greater severity, other studies have not been able to validate this finding.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kenyon KM, Eaton WO |title=Age at child obsessive-compulsive disorder onset and its relation to gender, symptom severity, and family functioning |journal=Archives of Scientific Psychology |date=16 November 2015 |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=150–158 |doi=10.1037/arc0000022|doi-access=free |hdl=1993/30297 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Looking at women specifically, a different study suggested that 62% of participants found that their symptoms worsened at a premenstrual age. Across the board, all demographics and studies showed a [[mean]] age of onset of less than 25.<ref name="Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders">{{Cite web |title=About OCD |url=https://med.stanford.edu/ocd/about.html |access-date=13 December 2022 |website=Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders |language=sm}}</ref>

Some OCD subtypes have been associated with improvement in performance on certain tasks, such as [[pattern recognition (psychology)|pattern recognition]] (washing subtype) and [[spatial working memory]] (obsessive thought subtype). Subgroups have also been distinguished by [[neuroimaging]] findings and treatment response, though neuroimaging studies have not been comprehensive enough to draw conclusions. Subtype-dependent treatment response has been studied and the hoarding subtype has consistently been least responsive to treatment.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = McKay D, Abramowitz JS, Calamari JE, Kyrios M, Radomsky A, Sookman D, Taylor S, Wilhelm S | title = A critical evaluation of obsessive-compulsive disorder subtypes: symptoms versus mechanisms | journal = Clinical Psychology Review | volume = 24 | issue = 3 | pages = 283–313 | date = July 2004 | pmid = 15245833 | doi = 10.1016/j.cpr.2004.04.003 }}</ref>

While OCD is considered a [[homogeneous]] disorder from a [[Neuropsychology|neuropsychological]] perspective, many of the symptoms may be the result of [[Comorbidity|comorbid]] disorders. For example, adults with OCD have exhibited more symptoms of [[attention deficit hyperactivity disorder]] (ADHD) and [[Autism spectrum|autism spectrum disorder]] (ASD) than adults without OCD.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Anholt GE, Cath DC, van Oppen P, Eikelenboom M, Smit JH, van Megen H, van Balkom AJ | title = Autism and ADHD symptoms in patients with OCD: are they associated with specific OC symptom dimensions or OC symptom severity? | journal = Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders | volume = 40 | issue = 5 | pages = 580–589 | date = May 2010 | pmid = 20039111 | pmc = 2855859 | doi = 10.1007/s10803-009-0922-1 }}</ref>

=== Obsessions ===
{{Main|Intrusive thought}}

{{See also|Primarily obsessional obsessive–compulsive disorder}}
[[File:Hendrick Andriessen - Vanity Piece - 1914-DE - Museum of Fine Arts Ghent (MSK).jpg|thumb|People with [[scrupulosity]] OCD may face intrusive thoughts such as worrying about [[death]].]]

Obsessions are stress-inducing thoughts that recur and persist, despite efforts to ignore or confront them.<ref name="Review10">{{cite journal |vauthors=Markarian Y, Larson MJ, Aldea MA, Baldwin SA, Good D, Berkeljon A, Murphy TK, Storch EA, McKay D |title=Multiple pathways to functional impairment in obsessive-compulsive disorder |journal=Clinical Psychology Review |volume=30 |issue=1 |pages=78–88 |date=February 2010 |pmid=19853982 |doi=10.1016/j.cpr.2009.09.005 |url=https://scholarsarchive.byu.edu/facpub/6053 }}</ref> People with OCD frequently perform tasks, or [[Compulsive behavior|compulsions]], to seek relief from obsession-related anxiety. Within and among individuals, initial obsessions vary in clarity and vividness. A relatively vague obsession could involve a general sense of disarray or tension, accompanied by a belief that life cannot proceed as normal while the imbalance remains. A more intense obsession could be a preoccupation with the thought or image of a close family member or friend dying, or intrusive thoughts related to [[Relationship obsessive–compulsive disorder|relationship rightness]].<ref name="Doron2013">{{cite journal | vauthors = Doron G, Szepsenwol O, Karp E, Gal N | title = Obsessing about intimate-relationships: testing the double relationship-vulnerability hypothesis | journal = Journal of Behavior Therapy and Experimental Psychiatry | volume = 44 | issue = 4 | pages = 433–440 | date = December 2013 | pmid = 23792752 | doi = 10.1016/j.jbtep.2013.05.003 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book| vauthors = Baer L |title= The Imp of the Mind: Exploring the Silent Epidemic of Obsessive Bad Thoughts |location= New York |publisher= Dutton |year= 2001 | isbn= 978-0-525-94562-8 |url= https://archive.org/details/impofmind00leeb|url-access=registration |page=xiv}}</ref> Other obsessions concern the possibility that someone or something other than oneself—such as [[God]], the [[devil]], or [[disease]]—will harm either the patient or the people or things the patient cares about. Others with OCD may experience the sensation of invisible protrusions emanating from their bodies or feel that [[Animism|inanimate objects are ensouled]].<ref name="Mash">{{Cite book | vauthors = Mash EJ, Wolfe DA |title=Abnormal child psychology  |date=2005 |publisher=[[The Thomson Corporation|Thomson Wadsworth]] |isbn=978-1-305-10542-3 |edition = 3rd |location= [[Belmont, California]] |page=197}}</ref> Another common obsession is [[scrupulosity]], the pathological guilt/anxiety about moral or religious issues. In scrupulosity, a person's obsessions focus on moral or religious fears, such as the fear of being an evil person or the fear of divine retribution for sin, for example [[damnation|going to Hell]].<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Deacon B, Nelson EA |year=2008 |title=On the nature and treatment of scrupulosity |url=http://jrul.libraries.rutgers.edu/index.php/pcsp/article/view/932/2329 |format=PDF |journal=Pragmatic Case Studies in Psychotherapy |volume=4 |issue=2 |pages=39–53 |doi=10.14713/pcsp.v4i2.932 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Huppert">{{cite journal |vauthors=Huppert JD, Siev J, Kushner ES |date=October 2007 |title=When religion and obsessive-compulsive disorder collide: treating scrupulosity in Ultra-Orthodox Jews |journal=Journal of Clinical Psychology |volume=63 |issue=10 |pages=925–41 |doi=10.1002/jclp.20404 |pmid=17828763}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.treatmyocd.com/what-is-ocd/common-fears/fear-of-going-to-hell-ocd|title=Fear of going to hell|first=Melanie Dideriksen, LPC|last=CAADC|website=NOCD}}</ref> [[Mysophobia]], a [[pathological fear]] of contamination and [[Microorganism|germs]], is another common obsession theme.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mysophobia (Germophobia): Causes, Symptoms & Treatment |url=https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/22436-mysophobia-germophobia |access-date=31 March 2023 |website=Cleveland Clinic |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book | vauthors = Hammond WA, Morton WJ |url= http://archive.org/details/neurologicalcon00mortgoog |title=Neurological contributions |date=1879 |publisher=New York, G. P. Putnam's sons |others=unknown library}}</ref>

Some people with OCD experience [[sexual obsessions]] that may involve intrusive thoughts or images of various sexual acts with strangers, acquaintances, relatives, animals, or religious figures and can include [[Heterosexuality|heterosexual]] or [[Homosexuality|homosexual]] contact with people of any age.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Chaudhary |first1=Sonam |last2=Singh |first2=Anand Pratap |last3=Varshney |first3=Aakriti |date=April 2022 |title=Psychodynamic Perspective of Sexual Obsessions in Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder |journal=Annals of Neurosciences |volume=29 |issue=2–3 |pages=159–165 |doi=10.1177/09727531221115305 |pmc=9676338 |pmid=36419523 |quote=OCD can occur in a wide range of subtypes, which may include sexual OCD, i.e., the occurrence of intrusive sexual thoughts. People with sexual OCD may experience unwanted obsessive sexual thoughts about a family member, dead or inanimate objects, animals, God, or children that can range from mild to severe. [...] Sexually oriented OCD is generally characterized by unwanted intrusive sexual thoughts and ego-dystonic sexual content, which may include thoughts about sexual activity with family members, child abuse, fears or thoughts related to sexual orientation, inappropriate sexual activity (e.g., with animals, children, or inanimate objects), and aggressive sexual behaviors.}}</ref><ref name="Osgood">{{Cite web |vauthors = Osgood-Hynes D |title=Thinking Bad Thoughts |url=http://www.raminader.com/PDFs%20Uploaded/OCD%20-%20Thinking%20Bad%20Thoughts.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111115230001/http://raminader.com/PDFs%20Uploaded/OCD%20-%20Thinking%20Bad%20Thoughts.pdf |archive-date=15 November 2011 |access-date=30 December 2006 |publisher=MGH/McLean OCD Institute |location=Belmont, Massachusetts}}</ref> Similar to other intrusive thoughts or images, some disquieting sexual thoughts are normal at times, but people with OCD may attach extraordinary significance to such thoughts. For example, obsessive fears about [[sexual orientation]] can appear to the affected individual, and even to those around them, as a crisis of [[sexual identity]].<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Allely CS, Pickard M |title=A systematic scoping review of the literature on sexual orientation obsessive compulsive disorder (SOOCD): Important clinical considerations and recommendations |journal=Psychiatry Res |volume=342 |issue= |article-number=116198 |date=December 2024 |pmid=39326275 |doi=10.1016/j.psychres.2024.116198 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="so-ocd">{{cite journal | vauthors = Williams MT, Farris SG | title = Sexual orientation obsessions in obsessive-compulsive disorder: prevalence and correlates | journal = Psychiatry Research | volume = 187 | issue = 1–2 | pages = 156–159 | date = May 2011 | pmid = 21094531 | pmc = 3070770 | doi = 10.1016/j.psychres.2010.10.019 | publisher = [[Elsevier]] }}</ref> Furthermore, the doubt that accompanies OCD leads to uncertainty regarding whether one might act on the troubling thoughts, resulting in self-criticism or self-loathing.<ref name="Osgood" />

Pedophilia-themed obsessive–compulsive disorder (also known as pedophile OCD or P-OCD) is an OCD subtype regarding reocurring compulsions and obsessions over one being a [[Pedophilia|pedophile]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bruce |first1=Simone Leavell |last2=Ching |first2=Terence H. W. |last3=Williams |first3=Monnica T. |date=2018 |title=Pedophilia-Themed Obsessive–Compulsive Disorder: Assessment, Differential Diagnosis, and Treatment with Exposure and Response Prevention |journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior |volume=47 |issue=2 |pages=389–402 |doi=10.1007/s10508-017-1031-4 |pmid=28822003 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bonagura |first1=Alexandra |last2=Abrams |first2=Dylan |last3=Teller |first3=Jonathan |date=2022 |title=Diagnostic Differential Between Pedophilic-OCD and Pedophilic Disorder: An Illustration with Two Vignettes |journal=Archives of Sexual Behavior |volume=51 |issue=4 |pages=2359–2368 |doi=10.1007/s10508-021-02273-5 |pmid=35445281 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ferreira |first1=Carla |last2=Ferreira |first2=Liliana |last3=Pombo |first3=Samuel |last4=Vieira |first4=Rui Xavier |date=1 October 2021 |title=Clinical Challenges in Pedophilia-Themed Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder |journal=Acta Médica Portuguesa |volume=34 |issue=10 |pages=692–695 |doi=10.20344/amp.13296 |pmid=33159729 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Munslow-Davies |first1=Samantha |last2=Anderson |first2=Rebecca A. |date=2022 |title=Effects of a brief education on psychologists' attitudes and diagnostic impressions of paedophilic obsessive-compulsive intrusive thoughts |journal=Journal of Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders |language=en |volume=32 |article-number=100707 |doi=10.1016/j.jocrd.2021.100707 |hdl=20.500.11937/87486 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Am I a Monster? An Overview of Common Features, Typical Course, Shame and Treatment of Pedophilia OCD (pOCD) |url=https://iocdf.org/expert-opinions/am-i-a-monster-an-overview-of-common-features-typical-course-shame-and-treatment-of-pedophilia-ocd-pocd/ |access-date=3 October 2024 |website=International OCD Foundation |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=24 January 2020 |title='My doctor mistook my OCD for paedophilia' |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/bbcthree/article/a534c571-035c-4423-9dd9-33c8d425f5f3 |access-date=3 October 2024 |website=BBC Three |language=en-GB}}</ref>

Most people with OCD understand that their thoughts do not correspond with reality; however, they feel that they must act as though these ideas are correct or realistic. For example, someone who engages in [[compulsive hoarding]] might be inclined to treat inorganic matter as if it had the sentience or rights of living organisms, despite accepting that such behavior is irrational on an intellectual level. There is debate as to whether hoarding should be considered an independent syndrome from OCD.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Murphy DL, Timpano KR, Wheaton MG, Greenberg BD, Miguel EC | title = Obsessive-compulsive disorder and its related disorders: a reappraisal of obsessive-compulsive spectrum concepts | journal = Dialogues in Clinical Neuroscience | volume = 12 | issue = 2 | pages = 131–148 | date = 2010 | pmid = 20623919 | pmc = 3181955 | doi = 10.31887/DCNS.2010.12.2/dmurphy | publisher = [[Taylor & Francis]] }}</ref>

=== Compulsions ===
{{Main|Compulsive behavior}}

[[File:OCD handwash.jpg|thumb|upright=1.35|A person exhibiting obsessive [[hand washing]]]]
Some people with OCD perform compulsive rituals because they inexplicably feel that they must do so, while others act compulsively to mitigate the anxiety that stems from obsessive thoughts. The affected individual might feel that these actions will either prevent a dreaded event from occurring or push the event from their thoughts. In any case, their reasoning is so [[Idiosyncrasy|idiosyncratic]] or distorted that it results in significant distress, either personally or for those around the affected individual. Excessive [[Excoriation disorder|skin picking]], [[Trichotillomania|hair pulling]], [[nail biting]] and other body-focused repetitive behavior disorders are all on the [[obsessive–compulsive spectrum]].<ref name="DSM52" /> Some individuals with OCD are aware that their behaviors are not rational, but they feel compelled to follow through with them to fend off feelings of panic or dread.<ref>{{Citation |title=Highlights of Changes from DSM-IV-TR to DSM-5 |url=http://www.dsm5.org/documents/changes%20from%20dsm-iv-tr%20to%20dsm-5.pdf |page=7 |year=2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131019204551/http://www.dsm5.org/Documents/changes%20from%20dsm-iv-tr%20to%20dsm-5.pdf |publisher=American Psychiatric Association |access-date=12 April 2016 |archive-date=19 October 2013 |url-status=live}}</ref> Furthermore, compulsions often stem from [[Memory distrust syndrome|memory distrust]], a symptom of OCD characterized by insecurity in one's skills in [[perception]], [[attention]] and [[memory]], even in cases where there is no clear evidence of a deficit.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hermans D, Engelen U, Grouwels L, Joos E, Lemmens J, Pieters G | title = Cognitive confidence in obsessive-compulsive disorder: distrusting perception, attention and memory | journal = Behaviour Research and Therapy | volume = 46 | issue = 1 | pages = 98–113 | date = January 2008 | pmid = 18076865 | doi = 10.1016/j.brat.2007.11.001 | publisher = [[Elsevier]] }}</ref>

Common compulsions may include hand washing, cleaning, checking things (such as locks on doors), repeating actions (such as repeatedly turning on and off switches), ordering items in a certain way and requesting reassurance.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3UUuXw7ISM0C&pg=PA418 |title=Psychiatric Nursing: Contemporary Practice |vauthors=Boyd MA |publisher=[[Lippincott Williams & Wilkins]] |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-397-55178-1 |location=Baltimore, Maryland}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Boyd MA | title = Integration of psychosocial rehabilitation into psychiatric nursing practice | journal = Issues in Mental Health Nursing | volume = 15 | issue = 1 | pages = 13–26 | date = 1994 | pmid = 8119793 | doi = 10.3109/01612849409074930 }}</ref> Although some individuals perform actions repeatedly, they do not necessarily perform these actions compulsively; for example, morning or nighttime routines and religious practices are not usually compulsions. Whether behaviors qualify as compulsions or mere habit depends on the context in which they are performed. For instance, arranging and ordering books for eight hours a day would be expected of someone who works in a library, but this routine would seem abnormal in other situations. In other words, habits tend to bring efficiency to one's life, while compulsions tend to disrupt it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder, (2005) |url=http://www.webmd.com/anxiety-panic/guide/obsessive-compulsive-disorder. |access-date=15 December 2009}}</ref> Furthermore, compulsions are different from [[tics]] (such as touching, tapping, rubbing or blinking) and [[stereotypic movement disorder|stereotyped movements]] (such as head banging, body rocking or self-biting), which are usually not as complex and not precipitated by obsessions.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Storch EA, Stigge-Kaufman D, Marien WE, Sajid M, Jacob ML, Geffken GR, Goodman WK, Murphy TK | title = Obsessive-compulsive disorder in youth with and without a chronic tic disorder | journal = Depression and Anxiety | volume = 25 | issue = 9 | pages = 761–767 | year = 2008 | pmid = 17345600 | doi = 10.1002/da.20304 | doi-access =  }}</ref> It can sometimes be difficult to tell the difference between compulsions and complex tics, and about 10–40% of people with OCD also have a lifetime tic disorder.<ref name="DSM52" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Conelea CA, Walther MR, Freeman JB, Garcia AM, Sapyta J, Khanna M, Franklin M | title = Tic-related obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD): phenomenology and treatment outcome in the Pediatric OCD Treatment Study II | journal = Journal of the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry | volume = 53 | issue = 12 | pages = 1308–1316 | date = December 2014 | pmid = 25457929 | pmc = 4254546 | doi = 10.1016/j.jaac.2014.09.014 }}</ref>

People with OCD rely on compulsions as an escape from their obsessive thoughts; however, they are aware that relief is only temporary and that intrusive thoughts will return. Some affected individuals use compulsions to avoid situations that may trigger obsessions. Compulsions may be actions directly related to the obsession, such as someone obsessed with contamination compulsively washing their hands, but they can be unrelated as well.<ref name="psychiatry.org" /> In addition to experiencing the anxiety and fear that typically accompanies OCD, affected individuals may spend hours performing compulsions every day. In such situations, it can become difficult for the person to fulfill their work, familial or social roles. These behaviors can also cause adverse physical symptoms; for example, people who obsessively wash their hands with [[antibacterial soap]] and hot water can make their skin red and raw with [[dermatitis]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hygiene of the Skin: When Is Clean Too Clean? Subtopic: "Skin Barrier Properties and Effect of Hand Hygiene Practices", Paragraph 5. |url=https://www.cdc.gov/ncidod/eid/vol7no2/larson.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090421205614/http://www.cdc.gov/ncidod/eid/vol7no2/larson.htm |archive-date=21 April 2009 |access-date=26 March 2009 |url-status=live}}</ref>

Individuals with OCD often use [[Rationalization (psychology)|rationalizations]] to explain their behavior; however, these rationalizations do not apply to the behavioral pattern, but to each individual occurrence. For example, someone compulsively checking the front door may argue that the time and stress associated with one check is less than the time and stress associated with being robbed, and checking is consequently the better option. This reasoning often occurs in a cyclical manner and can continue for as long as the affected person needs it to in order to feel safe.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pélissier MC, O'Connor KP | title = Deductive and inductive reasoning in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = The British Journal of Clinical Psychology | volume = 41 | issue = Pt 1 | pages = 15–27 | date = March 2002 | pmid = 11931675 | doi = 10.1348/014466502163769 | publisher = [[Wiley-Blackwell]] }}</ref>

OCD sometimes manifests in mental instead of overt compulsions. This manifestation may be termed "[[primarily obsessional OCD]]" and typically involves mental compulsions, such as mental avoidance or excessive rumination.<ref name="Williams_2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Williams MT, Farris SG, Turkheimer E, Pinto A, Ozanick K, Franklin ME, Liebowitz M, Simpson HB, Foa EB | title = Myth of the pure obsessional type in obsessive--compulsive disorder | journal = Depression and Anxiety | volume = 28 | issue = 6 | pages = 495–500 | date = June 2011 | pmid = 21509914 | pmc = 3227121 | doi = 10.1002/da.20820 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name="books.google.com">{{Cite book | vauthors = Toates FM, Toates F, Coschug-Toates O |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mOH0L6NYg6kC&pg=PA111 |title=Obsessive Compulsive Disorder: Practical, Tried-and-tested Strategies to Overcome OCD |date=2002 |publisher=Class Publishing Ltd |isbn=978-1-85959-069-0 |language=en}}</ref> OCD without overt compulsions could, by one estimate, characterize as many as 50–60% of OCD cases.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Weisman MM |date=May 1998 |title=The cross national epidemiology of obsessive–compulsive disorder |journal=New Developments in Obsessive-Compulsive and Spectrum Disorders |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=6–9}}</ref>

=== Insight and overvalued ideation ===
The [[DSM-5|Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders]] (DSM-5) identifies a continuum for the level of insight in OCD, ranging from good insight (the least severe) to no insight (the most severe). Good or fair insight is characterized by the acknowledgment that obsessive–compulsive beliefs are not or may not be true, while poor insight is characterized by the belief that obsessive–compulsive beliefs are probably true. The absence of insight altogether, in which the individual is completely convinced that their beliefs are true, is also identified as a [[delusion]]al thought pattern and occurs in about 4% of people with OCD.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration |date=June 2016 |title=Table 3.13, DSM-IV to DSM-5 Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder Comparison |url=https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK519704/table/ch3.t13/ |access-date=11 December 2021 |website=Impact of the DSM-IV to DSM-5 Changes on the National Survey on Drug Use and Health [Internet] |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sadock">{{Cite book | vauthors = Ruiz P, Sadock B, Sadock V |title=Kaplan and Sadock's Comprehensive Textbook of Psychiatry|publisher=LWW |year=2017 |isbn=978-1-4511-0047-1 |edition=10th}}</ref> When cases of OCD with no insight become severe, affected individuals have an unshakable belief in the reality of their delusions, which can make their cases difficult to differentiate from [[psychosis|psychotic disorders]].<ref name="pmid10755077">{{cite journal | vauthors = O'Dwyer AM, Marks I | title = Obsessive-compulsive disorder and delusions revisited | journal = The British Journal of Psychiatry: The Journal of Mental Science | volume = 176 | issue = 3| pages = 281–4 | date = March 2000 | pmid = 10755077 | doi = 10.1192/bjp.176.3.281 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Additionally, good insight can include cases where the individual has no insight during the experience but demonstrates insight later, when they are in a calmer state of mind.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rasmussen |first1=Andreas Rosén |last2=Parnas |first2=Josef |title=What is obsession? Differentiating obsessive-compulsive disorder and the schizophrenia spectrum |journal=Schizophrenia Research |date=May 2022 |volume=243 |pages=1–8 |doi=10.1016/j.schres.2022.02.014 |pmid=35219003 |doi-access=free |url=https://researchprofiles.ku.dk/da/publications/b4e86acb-4aae-46ef-a463-fb2891fae891 }}</ref>

Some people with OCD exhibit what is known as ''overvalued ideas'', ideas that are abnormal compared to affected individuals' respective cultures, and more treatment-resistant than most negative thoughts and obsessions.<ref name="Veale_2002" /> After some discussion, it is possible to convince the individual that their fears are unfounded. It may be more difficult to practice [[Exposure therapy#Obsessive compulsive disorder|exposure and response prevention therapy]] (ERP) on such people, as they may be unwilling to cooperate, at least initially.<ref name="Obsessions, overvalued ideas, and d">{{cite journal | vauthors = Kozak MJ, Foa EB | title = Obsessions, overvalued ideas, and delusions in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Behaviour Research and Therapy | volume = 32 | issue = 3 | pages = 343–353 | date = March 1994 | pmid = 8192634 | doi = 10.1016/0005-7967(94)90132-5 }}</ref> Similar to how insight is identified on a continuum, obsessive–compulsive beliefs are characterized on a spectrum, ranging from obsessive doubt to delusional conviction. In the United States, overvalued ideation (OVI) is considered most akin to poor insight—especially when considering belief strength as one of an idea's key identifiers.<ref name="Veale_2002"/> Furthermore, severe and frequent overvalued ideas are considered similar to ''idealized values'', which are so rigidly held by, and so important to affected individuals, that they end up becoming a defining identity.<ref name="Veale_2002">{{cite journal | vauthors = Veale D | title = Over-valued ideas: a conceptual analysis | journal = Behaviour Research and Therapy | volume = 40 | issue = 4 | pages = 383–400 | date = April 2002 | pmid = 12002896 | doi = 10.1016/S0005-7967(01)00016-X }}</ref> In adolescent OCD patients, OVI is considered a severe symptom.<ref name="Borda_2017">{{cite journal | vauthors = Borda T, Neziroglu F, Taboas W, McKay D, Frenkiel L | title = Overvalued ideation in adolescents with obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Psychiatry Research | volume = 255 | pages = 66–71 | date = September 2017 | pmid = 28528243 | doi = 10.1016/j.psychres.2017.05.001 }}</ref>

Historically, OVI has been thought to be linked to poorer treatment outcome in patients with OCD, but it is currently considered a poor indicator of prognosis.<ref name="Borda_2017" /><ref name="Obsessions, overvalued ideas, and d"/> The Overvalued Ideas Scale (OVIS) has been developed as a reliable quantitative method of measuring levels of OVI in patients with OCD. Research has suggested that overvalued ideas are more stable for those with more extreme OVIS scores.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Neziroglu F, McKay D, Yaryura-Tobias JA, Stevens KP, Todaro J | title = The Overvalued Ideas Scale: development, reliability and validity in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Behaviour Research and Therapy | volume = 37 | issue = 9 | pages = 881–902 | date = September 1999 | pmid = 10458051 | doi = 10.1016/S0005-7967(98)00191-0 }}</ref>

=== Cognitive performance ===
Though OCD was once believed to be associated with above-average intelligence, this does not appear to necessarily be the case.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Abramovitch A, Anholt G, Raveh-Gottfried S, Hamo N, Abramowitz JS | title = Meta-Analysis of Intelligence Quotient (IQ) in Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder | journal = Neuropsychology Review | volume = 28 | issue = 1 | pages = 111–120 | date = March 2018 | pmid = 28864868 | doi = 10.1007/s11065-017-9358-0 }}</ref> A 2013 review reported that people with OCD may sometimes have mild but wide-ranging [[cognitive deficit]]s, most significantly those affecting [[spatial memory]] and to a lesser extent with [[verbal memory]], [[Verbal fluency test|fluency]], [[Executive functions|executive function]] and [[Cognitive processing speed|processing speed]], while auditory attention was not significantly affected.<ref name="shin">{{cite journal | vauthors = Shin NY, Lee TY, Kim E, Kwon JS | title = Cognitive functioning in obsessive-compulsive disorder: a meta-analysis | journal = Psychological Medicine | volume = 44 | issue = 6 | pages = 1121–1130 | date = April 2014 | pmid = 23866289 | doi = 10.1017/S0033291713001803 }}</ref> People with OCD show impairment in formulating an organizational strategy for coding information, [[set-shifting]], and motor and [[cognitive inhibition]].<ref name="Aydin">{{cite journal | vauthors = Çetinay Aydın P, Güleç Öyekçin D | title = [Cognitive functions in patients with obsessive compulsive disorder] | journal = Turk Psikiyatri Dergisi = Turkish Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 24 | issue = 4 | pages = 266–274 | year = 2013 | pmid = 24310094 | doi = 10.5080/u7172 }}</ref>

Specific subtypes of symptom dimensions in OCD have been associated with specific cognitive deficits.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pauls DL, Abramovitch A, Rauch SL, Geller DA | title = Obsessive-compulsive disorder: an integrative genetic and neurobiological perspective | journal = Nature Reviews. Neuroscience | volume = 15 | issue = 6 | pages = 410–424 | date = June 2014 | pmid = 24840803 | doi = 10.1038/nrn3746 }}</ref> For example, the results of one [[meta-analysis]] comparing washing and checking symptoms reported that washers outperformed checkers on eight out of ten cognitive tests.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Leopold R, Backenstrass M | title = Neuropsychological differences between obsessive-compulsive washers and checkers: a systematic review and meta-analysis | journal = Journal of Anxiety Disorders | volume = 30 | pages = 48–58 | date = March 2015 | pmid = 25601381 | doi = 10.1016/j.janxdis.2014.12.016 }}</ref> The symptom dimension of contamination and cleaning may be associated with higher scores on tests of inhibition and verbal memory.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Abramovitch A, Cooperman A |date=April 2015 |title=The cognitive neuropsychology of obsessive-compulsive disorder: A critical review |journal=Journal of Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders |volume=5 |pages=24–36 |doi=10.1016/j.jocrd.2015.01.002}}</ref>

=== Pediatric OCD ===
Approximately 1–2% of children are affected by OCD.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Jones AM, De Nadai AS, Arnold EB, McGuire JF, Lewin AB, Murphy TK, Storch EA | title = Psychometric properties of the obsessive compulsive inventory: child version in children and adolescents with obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Child Psychiatry and Human Development | volume = 44 | issue = 1 | pages = 137–151 | date = February 2013 | pmid = 22711294 | doi = 10.1007/s10578-012-0315-0 }}</ref> An international study showed that OCD most often develops during childhood or adolescence, with 21% of subjects having developed symptoms during childhood (age 12 and under) and another 36% having developed symptoms during adolescence (ages 13-17).<ref name="Steele 2024">{{cite report |last1=Steele |first1=Dale W. |last2=Caputo |first2=Eduardo L. |last3=Kanaan |first3=Ghid |last4=Zahradnik |first4=Michael L. |last5=Brannon |first5=Elizabeth |last6=Freeman |first6=Jennifer B. |last7=Balk |first7=Ethan M. |last8=Trikalinos |first8=Thomas A. |last9=Adam |first9=Gaelen P. |title=Diagnosis and Management of Obsessive Compulsive Disorders in Children |date=2024 |id={{NCBIBook|NBK611136}} |doi=10.23970/AHRQEPCCER276 |pmid=39836793 }}</ref> OCD diagnosis in children occurs at similar rates across different ethnic groups and races, but African American children are less likely to receive treatment.<ref name="Steele 2024" /> The clinical presentation of OCD in children shares many similarities with that observed in adults. OCD is considered a highly familial disorder, with a phenotypic heritability of around 50%. Symptoms tend to develop more frequently in children 10–14 years of age, with males displaying symptoms at an earlier age, and at a more severe level than females.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Last CG, Strauss CC |year=1989 |title=Obsessive—compulsive disorder in childhood |journal=Journal of Anxiety Disorders |volume=3 |issue=4 |pages=295–302 |doi=10.1016/0887-6185(89)90020-0}}</ref> In children, symptoms can be grouped into at least four types, including sporadic and tic-related OCD.<ref name="Leckman" />  

The Children's Yale–Brown Obsessive–Compulsive Scale (CY-BOCS) is the gold standard measure for assessment of pediatric OCD.<ref name="Evidence-Based Assessment of Obsess">{{cite journal | vauthors = Rapp AM, Bergman RL, Piacentini J, McGuire JF | title = Evidence-Based Assessment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder | journal = Journal of Central Nervous System Disease | volume = 8 | pages = 13–29 | date = January 2016 | pmid = 27594793 | pmc = 4994744 | doi = 10.4137/JCNSD.S38359 | doi-access = free }}</ref> It follows the Y-BOCS format, but with a Symptom Checklist that is adapted for developmental appropriateness. Insight, avoidance, indecisiveness, responsibility, pervasive slowness and doubting are not included in a rating of overall severity. The CY-BOCS has demonstrated good convergent validity with clinician-rated OCD severity and good to fair discriminant validity from measures of closely related anxiety, depression and tic severity.<ref name="Evidence-Based Assessment of Obsess"/> The CY-BOCS Total Severity score is an important monitoring tool as it is responsive to pharmacotherapy and psychotherapy.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = McGuire JF, Piacentini J, Lewin AB, Brennan EA, Murphy TK, Storch EA | title = A Meta-Analysis of Cognitive Behavior Therapy and Medication for Child Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: Moderators of Treatment Efficacy, Response, and Remission | journal = Depression and Anxiety | volume = 32 | issue = 8 | pages = 580–593 | date = August 2015 | pmid = 26130211 | pmc = 4515191 | doi = 10.1002/da.22389 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = McGuire JF, Lewin AB, Horng B, Murphy TK, Storch EA | title = The nature, assessment, and treatment of obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Postgraduate Medicine | volume = 124 | issue = 1 | pages = 152–165 | date = January 2012 | pmid = 22314125 | doi = 10.3810/pgm.2012.01.2528 }}</ref> Positive treatment response is characterized by 25% reduction in CY-BOCS total score and diagnostic remission is associated with a 45%-50% reduction in Total Severity score (or a score <15).<ref name="Evidence-Based Assessment of Obsess"/> The Child Behavior Checklist-Obsessive Compulsive Subscale (CBCL-OCS) appears to demonstrate sufficient accuracy in identifying children and adolescents who may require additional evaluation for OCD.<ref name="Steele 2024" />

The Children's Florida Obsessive Compulsive Inventory (C-FOCI) utilizes 17 specific, brief questions which focus on compulsions or obsessions frequent among younger individuals with OCD. These act as a dichotomous tool to evaluate whether obsessions and compulsions are present or absent, using a symptom checklist on a scale of 0 to 17, and a severity scale of 0 to 85.<ref name="Steele 2024"/>

[[Cognitive behavioral therapy]] (CBT) is the first line treatment for mild to moderate cases of OCD in children, while medication plus CBT is recommended for moderate to severe cases.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Watson HJ, Rees CS | title = Meta-analysis of randomized, controlled treatment trials for pediatric obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, and Allied Disciplines | volume = 49 | issue = 5 | pages = 489–498 | date = May 2008 | pmid = 18400058 | doi = 10.1111/j.1469-7610.2007.01875.x }}</ref><ref name="Cognitive-Behavior Therapy, Sertral">{{cite journal | title = Cognitive-behavior therapy, sertraline, and their combination for children and adolescents with obsessive-compulsive disorder: the Pediatric OCD Treatment Study (POTS) randomized controlled trial | journal = JAMA | volume = 292 | issue = 16 | pages = 1969–1976 | date = October 2004 | pmid = 15507582 | doi = 10.1001/jama.292.16.1969 | author1 = Pediatric OCD Treatment Study (POTS) Team }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Geller DA, Biederman J, Stewart SE, Mullin B, Martin A, Spencer T, Faraone SV | title = Which SSRI? A meta-analysis of pharmacotherapy trials in pediatric obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = The American Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 160 | issue = 11 | pages = 1919–1928 | date = November 2003 | pmid = 14594734 | doi = 10.1176/appi.ajp.160.11.1919 }}</ref> Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors ([[SSRI]]s) are first-line medications for OCD in children with established AACAP guidelines for dosing.<ref name="Practice Parameter for the Assessme">{{cite journal | title = Practice parameter for the assessment and treatment of children and adolescents with obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Journal of the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry | volume = 51 | issue = 1 | pages = 98–113 | date = January 2012 | pmid = 22176943 | doi = 10.1016/j.jaac.2011.09.019 | doi-access =  | vauthors = Geller DA, March J }}</ref> Medication in addition to a CBT intervention like [[Exposure therapy|exposure and response prevention (ERP)]] is more beneficial than only using medication in the treatment of OCD in children.<ref name="Steele 2024" />

=== Associated conditions ===
People with OCD may be diagnosed with other conditions as well, such as obsessive–compulsive personality disorder, [[major depressive disorder]], [[bipolar disorder]], [[generalized anxiety disorder]], [[anorexia nervosa]], [[social anxiety disorder]], [[bulimia nervosa]], [[Tourette syndrome]], [[transformation obsession]], ASD, ADHD, [[dermatillomania]], [[body dysmorphic disorder]] and [[trichotillomania]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Chen YW, Dilsaver SC | title = Comorbidity for obsessive-compulsive disorder in bipolar and unipolar disorders | journal = Psychiatry Research | volume = 59 | issue = 1–2 | pages = 57–64 | date = November 1995 | pmid = 8771221 | doi = 10.1016/0165-1781(95)02752-1 }}</ref> More than 50% of people with OCD experience suicidal tendencies and 15% have attempted [[suicide]].<ref name="Fenske_2009" /> Depression, anxiety and prior suicide attempts increase the risk of future suicide attempts.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Angelakis I, Gooding P, Tarrier N, Panagioti M | title = Suicidality in obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD): a systematic review and meta-analysis | journal = Clinical Psychology Review | volume = 39 | pages = 1–15 | date = July 2015 | pmid = 25875222 | doi = 10.1016/j.cpr.2015.03.002 }}</ref>

It has been found that between 18 and 34% of females currently experiencing OCD scored positively on an inventory measuring disordered eating.<ref name="Bang_2020">{{cite journal | vauthors = Bang L, Kristensen UB, Wisting L, Stedal K, Garte M, Minde Å, Rø Ø | title = Presence of eating disorder symptoms in patients with obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = BMC Psychiatry | volume = 20 | issue = 1 | article-number = 36 | date = January 2020 | pmid = 32000754 | pmc = 6993325 | doi = 10.1186/s12888-020-2457-0 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Another study found that 7% are likely to have an [[eating disorder]],<ref name="Bang_2020" /> while another found that fewer than 5% of males have OCD and an eating disorder.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Tyagi H, Patel R, Rughooputh F, Abrahams H, Watson AJ, Drummond L |title=Comparative Prevalence of Eating Disorders in Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder and Other Anxiety Disorders |journal=Psychiatry Journal |year=2015 |volume=2015 |article-number=186927 |doi=10.1155/2015/186927 |pmid=26366407 |pmc=4561118 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

Individuals with OCD have also been found to be affected by [[delayed sleep phase disorder]] at a substantially higher rate than the general public.<ref name="Turner 2007">{{cite journal | vauthors = Turner J, Drummond LM, Mukhopadhyay S, Ghodse H, White S, Pillay A, Fineberg NA | title = A prospective study of delayed sleep phase syndrome in patients with severe resistant obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = World Psychiatry | volume = 6 | issue = 2 | pages = 108–111 | date = June 2007 | pmid = 18235868 | pmc = 2219909 }}</ref><ref name="Paterson2013"/> Moreover, severe OCD symptoms are consistently associated with greater [[sleep disturbance]]. Reduced total sleep time and sleep efficiency have been observed in people with OCD, with delayed sleep onset and offset.<ref name="Paterson2013">{{cite journal | vauthors = Paterson JL, Reynolds AC, Ferguson SA, Dawson D | title = Sleep and obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) | journal = Sleep Medicine Reviews | volume = 17 | issue = 6 | pages = 465–474 | date = December 2013 | pmid = 23499210 | doi = 10.1016/j.smrv.2012.12.002 }}</ref>

Some research has demonstrated a link between [[Addiction|drug addiction]] and OCD. For example, there is a higher risk of drug addiction among those with any [[anxiety disorder]], likely as a way of [[Coping (psychology)|coping]] with the heightened levels of anxiety. However, drug addiction among people with OCD may be a compulsive behavior. Depression is also extremely prevalent among people with OCD. One explanation for the high depression rate among OCD populations was posited by Mineka, Watson and Clark (1998), who explained that people with OCD, or any other anxiety disorder, may feel "out of control".<ref name="comorbidity">{{cite journal | vauthors = Mineka S, Watson D, Clark LA | title = Comorbidity of anxiety and unipolar mood disorders | journal = Annual Review of Psychology | volume = 49 | pages = 377–412 | year = 1998 | pmid = 9496627 | doi = 10.1146/annurev.psych.49.1.377 }}</ref>

Someone exhibiting OCD signs does not necessarily have OCD. Behaviors that present as obsessive–compulsive can also be found in a number of other conditions, including [[obsessive–compulsive personality disorder]] (OCPD), autism spectrum disorder (ASD) or disorders in which [[perseveration]] is a possible feature (ADHD, [[PTSD]], bodily disorders or [[Stereotypy|stereotyped behaviors]]).<ref name="differential">[http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1826591-differential Pediatric Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder Differential Diagnoses] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120917123925/http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1826591-differential |date=17 September 2012 }} – 2012</ref> Some cases of OCD present symptoms typically associated with Tourette syndrome, such as compulsions that may appear to resemble [[Tic#Motor or phonic|motor tics]]; this has been termed ''tic-related OCD'' or ''Tourettic OCD''.<ref name="10.1176/appi.neuropsych.21.1.59">{{cite journal | vauthors = Mansueto CS, Keuler DJ | title = Tic or compulsion?: it's Tourettic OCD | journal = Behavior Modification | volume = 29 | issue = 5 | pages = 784–799 | date = September 2005 | pmid = 16046664 | doi = 10.1177/0145445505279261 }}</ref><ref name="OCD and T">{{Cite web |title=OCD and Tourette Syndrome: Re-examining the Relationship |url=http://www.ocfoundation.org/eo_ts.aspx |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131101085624/http://www.ocfoundation.org/eo_ts.aspx |archive-date=1 November 2013 |access-date=30 October 2013 |publisher=International OCD Foundation}}</ref>

OCD frequently occurs [[Comorbidity|comorbidly]] with both [[bipolar disorder]] and [[major depressive disorder]]. Between 60 and 80% of those with OCD experience a major depressive episode in their lifetime. Comorbidity rates have been reported at between 19 and 90%, as a result of methodological differences. Between 9–35% of those with bipolar disorder also have OCD, compared to 1–2% in the general population. About 50% of those with OCD experience [[Cyclothymia|cyclothymic]] traits or [[Hypomania|hypomanic]] episodes. OCD is also associated with anxiety disorders. Lifetime comorbidity for OCD has been reported at 22% for [[specific phobia]], 18% for [[social anxiety disorder]], 12% for [[panic disorder]] and 30% for [[generalized anxiety disorder]]. The [[comorbidity]] rate for OCD and ADHD has been reported to be as high as 51%.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pallanti S, Grassi G, Sarrecchia ED, Cantisani A, Pellegrini M | title = Obsessive-compulsive disorder comorbidity: clinical assessment and therapeutic implications | journal = [[Frontiers Media|Frontiers in Psychiatry]] | volume = 2 | page = 70 | date = 2011 | pmid = 22203806 | pmc = 3243905 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyt.2011.00070 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

== Causes ==
{{Main|Cause of obsessive–compulsive disorder}}

The cause of OCD is unknown.<ref name="NIH20152" /> Both environmental and genetic factors are believed to play a role. Risk factors include a history of [[adverse childhood experiences]] or other [[stress (psychological)|stress]]-inducing events.<ref name="DSM52" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Ou W, Li Z, Zheng Q, Chen W, Liu J, Liu B, Zhang Y | title = Association Between Childhood Maltreatment and Symptoms of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Meta-Analysis | journal = Frontiers in Psychiatry | volume = 11 | article-number = 612586 | date = 20 January 2021 | pmid = 33551875 | pmc = 7854900 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyt.2020.612586 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Gu W, Zhao Q, Yuan C, Yi Z, Zhao M, Wang Z | title = Impact of adverse childhood experiences on the symptom severity of different mental disorders: a cross-diagnostic study | journal = General Psychiatry | volume = 35 | issue = 2 | article-number = e100741 | date = 2022 | pmid = 35572774 | pmc = 9036421 | doi = 10.1136/gpsych-2021-100741 }}</ref>

=== Drug-induced OCD ===
Some medications, toxin exposures and drugs, such as [[methamphetamine]] or [[cocaine]], can induce obsessive–compulsive symptoms in people without a history of OCD. [[Atypical antipsychotic]]s such as [[olanzapine]] and [[clozapine]] can induce OCD in some people, particularly individuals with [[schizophrenia]].

The diagnostic criteria include:

# General OCD symptoms (obsessions, compulsions, skin picking, hair pulling, etc.) that developed soon after exposure to the substance or medication which can produce such symptoms.
# The onset of symptoms cannot be explained by an obsessive–compulsive and related disorder that is not substance/medication-induced and should last for a substantial period of time (about 1 month)
# This disturbance does not only occur during [[delirium]].
# Clinically induces distress or impairment in social, occupational or other important areas of functioning.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Alevizos B, Papageorgiou C, Christodoulou GN | title = Obsessive-compulsive symptoms with olanzapine | journal = The International Journal of Neuropsychopharmacology | volume = 7 | issue = 3 | pages = 375–377 | date = September 2004 | pmid = 15231024 | doi = 10.1017/S1461145704004456 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kulkarni G, Narayanaswamy JC, Math SB | title = Olanzapine induced de-novo obsessive compulsive disorder in a patient with schizophrenia | journal = Indian Journal of Pharmacology | volume = 44 | issue = 5 | pages = 649–650 | date = 1 January 2012 | pmid = 23112432 | pmc = 3480803 | doi = 10.4103/0253-7613.100406 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Lykouras L, Zervas IM, Gournellis R, Malliori M, Rabavilas A | title = Olanzapine and obsessive-compulsive symptoms | journal = European Neuropsychopharmacology | volume = 10 | issue = 5 | pages = 385–387 | date = September 2000 | pmid = 10974610 | doi = 10.1016/s0924-977x(00)00096-1 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Schirmbeck F, Zink M | title = Clozapine-induced obsessive-compulsive symptoms in schizophrenia: a critical review | journal = Current Neuropharmacology | volume = 10 | issue = 1 | pages = 88–95 | date = March 2012 | pmid = 22942882 | pmc = 3286851 | doi = 10.2174/157015912799362724 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Substance/Medication-Induced Obsessive-Compulsive and related disorder |url=https://www.psychdb.com/ocd/z-substance-medication |website=PsychDB |date=2 February 2022 |access-date=28 February 2024}}</ref>

=== Genetics ===
There appear to be some [[genetics|genetic]] components of OCD causation, with [[identical twins]] more often affected than fraternal twins.<ref name="DSM52" /> Furthermore, individuals with OCD are more likely to have first-degree family members exhibiting the same disorders than [[matched control]]s. In cases in which OCD develops during childhood, there is a much stronger familial link in the disorder than with cases in which OCD develops later in adulthood. OCD is highly familial, about 50% heritable.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Blanco-Vieira |first1=Thiago |last2=Radua |first2=Joaquim |last3=Marcelino |first3=Lívia |last4=Bloch |first4=Michael |last5=Mataix-Cols |first5=David |last6=do Rosário |first6=Maria Conceição |date=2023-06-28 |title=The genetic epidemiology of obsessive-compulsive disorder: a systematic review and meta-analysis |journal=Translational Psychiatry |language=en |volume=13 |issue=1 |page=230 |doi=10.1038/s41398-023-02433-2 |pmid=37380645 |pmc=10307810 }}</ref> OCD is believed to be a heterogeneous disorder.<ref name="Steele 2024" />

A genome-wide association study of OCD identified 30 significant [[Locus (genetics)|genetic loci]] and 25 likely causal genes, including [[WDR6]], DALRD3, and [[P120 catenin|CTNND1]], with ~11,500 variants explaining most of its heritability.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Strom |first1=Nora I. |last2=Gerring |first2=Zachary F. |last3=Galimberti |first3=Marco |last4=Yu |first4=Dongmei |last5=Halvorsen |first5=Matthew W. |last6=Abdellaoui |first6=Abdel |last7=Rodriguez-Fontenla |first7=Cristina |last8=Sealock |first8=Julia M. |last9=Bigdeli |first9=Tim |last10=Coleman |first10=Jonathan R. |last11=Mahjani |first11=Behrang |last12=Thorp |first12=Jackson G. |last13=Bey |first13=Katharina |last14=Burton |first14=Christie L. |last15=Luykx |first15=Jurjen J. |date=2025 |title=Genome-wide analyses identify 30 loci associated with obsessive–compulsive disorder |journal=Nature Genetics |language=en |volume=57 |issue=6 |pages=1389–1401 |doi=10.1038/s41588-025-02189-z |pmid=40360802 |pmc=12165847 }}</ref> Genetic risk is linked to [[Excitatory synapse|excitatory neurons]] in the [[hippocampus]] and [[cerebral cortex]], D1 and D2 medium spiny neurons, and overlaps with anxiety, depression, [[anorexia nervosa]], and [[Tourette syndrome]].<ref name=":1" />

Research has found there to be a genetic correlation between anorexia nervosa and OCD, suggesting a strong [[etiology]].<ref name="Lee_2020">{{cite journal | vauthors = Lee EB, Barney JL, Twohig MP, Lensegrav-Benson T, Quakenbush B | title = Obsessive compulsive disorder and thought action fusion: Relationships with eating disorder outcomes | journal = Eating Behaviors | volume = 37 | article-number = 101386 | date = April 2020 | pmid = 32388080 | doi = 10.1016/j.eatbeh.2020.101386 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Levinson CA, Brosof LC, Ram SS, Pruitt A, Russell S, Lenze EJ | title = Obsessions are strongly related to eating disorder symptoms in anorexia nervosa and atypical anorexia nervosa | journal = Eating Behaviors | volume = 34 | article-number = 101298 | date = August 2019 | pmid = 31176948 | pmc = 6708491 | doi = 10.1016/j.eatbeh.2019.05.001 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Meier M, Kossakowski JJ, Jones PJ, Kay B, Riemann BC, McNally RJ | title = Obsessive-compulsive symptoms in eating disorders: A network investigation | journal = The International Journal of Eating Disorders | volume = 53 | issue = 3 | pages = 362–371 | date = March 2020 | pmid = 31749199 | doi = 10.1002/eat.23196 }}</ref> First and second hand relatives of probands with OCD have a greater risk of developing anorexia nervosa as genetic relatedness increases.<ref name="Lee_2020" />

A [[mutation]] has been found in the human [[serotonin]] transporter gene [[hSERT]] in unrelated families with OCD.<ref name="pmid14593431">{{cite journal | vauthors = Ozaki N, Goldman D, Kaye WH, Plotnicov K, Greenberg BD, Lappalainen J, Rudnick G, Murphy DL | title = Serotonin transporter missense mutation associated with a complex neuropsychiatric phenotype | journal = Molecular Psychiatry | volume = 8 | issue = 11 | pages = 933–936 | date = November 2003 | pmid = 14593431 | doi = 10.1038/sj.mp.4001365 }}</ref>

A [[systematic review]] found that while neither [[allele]] was associated with OCD overall, in [[Caucasian race|Caucasians]], the L allele was associated with OCD.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bloch MH, Landeros-Weisenberger A, Sen S, Dombrowski P, Kelmendi B, Coric V, Pittenger C, Leckman JF | title = Association of the serotonin transporter polymorphism and obsessive-compulsive disorder: systematic review | journal = American Journal of Medical Genetics. Part B, Neuropsychiatric Genetics | volume = 147B | issue = 6 | pages = 850–858 | date = September 2008 | pmid = 18186076 | doi = 10.1002/ajmg.b.30699 }}</ref> Another meta-analysis observed an increased risk in those with the [[Zygosity|homozygous]] S allele, but found the LS [[genotype]] to be inversely associated with OCD.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Lin PY | title = Meta-analysis of the association of serotonin transporter gene polymorphism with obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Progress in Neuro-Psychopharmacology & Biological Psychiatry | volume = 31 | issue = 3 | pages = 683–689 | date = April 2007 | pmid = 17291658 | doi = 10.1016/j.pnpbp.2006.12.024 }}</ref>

A [[genome]]-wide association study found OCD to be linked with [[single-nucleotide polymorphism]]s (SNPs) near [[BTBD3]] and two SNPs in [[DLGAP1]] in a trio-based analysis, but no SNP reached significance when analyzed with [[Case control|case-control]] data.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Stewart SE, Yu D, Scharf JM, Neale BM, Fagerness JA, Mathews CA, Arnold PD, Evans PD, Gamazon ER, Davis LK, Osiecki L, McGrath L, Haddad S, Crane J, Hezel D, Illman C, Mayerfeld C, Konkashbaev A, Liu C, Pluzhnikov A, Tikhomirov A, Edlund CK, Rauch SL, Moessner R, Falkai P, Maier W, Ruhrmann S, Grabe HJ, Lennertz L, Wagner M, Bellodi L, Cavallini MC, Richter MA, Cook EH, Kennedy JL, Rosenberg D, Stein DJ, Hemmings SM, Lochner C, Azzam A, Chavira DA, Fournier E, Garrido H, Sheppard B, Umaña P, Murphy DL, Wendland JR, Veenstra-VanderWeele J, Denys D, Blom R, Deforce D, Van Nieuwerburgh F, Westenberg HG, Walitza S, Egberts K, Renner T, Miguel EC, Cappi C, Hounie AG, Conceição do Rosário M, Sampaio AS, Vallada H, Nicolini H, Lanzagorta N, Camarena B, Delorme R, Leboyer M, Pato CN, Pato MT, Voyiaziakis E, Heutink P, Cath DC, Posthuma D, Smit JH, Samuels J, Bienvenu OJ, Cullen B, Fyer AJ, Grados MA, Greenberg BD, McCracken JT, Riddle MA, Wang Y, Coric V, Leckman JF, Bloch M, Pittenger C, Eapen V, Black DW, Ophoff RA, Strengman E, Cusi D, Turiel M, Frau F, Macciardi F, Gibbs JR, Cookson MR, Singleton A, Hardy J, Crenshaw AT, Parkin MA, Mirel DB, Conti DV, Purcell S, Nestadt G, Hanna GL, Jenike MA, Knowles JA, Cox N, Pauls DL | title = Genome-wide association study of obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Molecular Psychiatry | volume = 18 | issue = 7 | pages = 788–798 | date = July 2013 | pmid = 22889921 | pmc = 4218751 | doi = 10.1038/mp.2012.85 }}</ref>

One meta-analysis found a small but significant association between a polymorphism in [[SLC1A1]] and OCD.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Stewart SE, Mayerfeld C, Arnold PD, Crane JR, O'Dushlaine C, Fagerness JA, Yu D, Scharf JM, Chan E, Kassam F, Moya PR, Wendland JR, Delorme R, Richter MA, Kennedy JL, Veenstra-VanderWeele J, Samuels J, Greenberg BD, McCracken JT, Knowles JA, Fyer AJ, Rauch SL, Riddle MA, Grados MA, Bienvenu OJ, Cullen B, Wang Y, Shugart YY, Piacentini J, Rasmussen S, Nestadt G, Murphy DL, Jenike MA, Cook EH, Pauls DL, Hanna GL, Mathews CA | title = Meta-analysis of association between obsessive-compulsive disorder and the 3' region of neuronal glutamate transporter gene SLC1A1 | journal = American Journal of Medical Genetics. Part B, Neuropsychiatric Genetics | volume = 162B | issue = 4 | pages = 367–379 | date = June 2013 | pmid = 23606572 | doi = 10.1002/ajmg.b.32137 | hdl-access = free | hdl = 2027.42/98412 }}</ref>

The relationship between OCD and [[Catechol-O-methyltransferase]] (COMT) has been inconsistent, with one meta-analysis reporting a significant association, albeit only in men, and another meta analysis reporting no association.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pooley EC, Fineberg N, Harrison PJ | title = The met(158) allele of catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) is associated with obsessive-compulsive disorder in men: case-control study and meta-analysis | journal = Molecular Psychiatry | volume = 12 | issue = 6 | pages = 556–561 | date = June 2007 | pmid = 17264842 | doi = 10.1038/sj.mp.4001951 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Azzam A, Mathews CA | title = Meta-analysis of the association between the catecholamine-O-methyl-transferase gene and obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = American Journal of Medical Genetics. Part B, Neuropsychiatric Genetics | volume = 123B | issue = 1 | pages = 64–69 | date = November 2003 | pmid = 14582147 | doi = 10.1002/ajmg.b.20013 }}</ref>

It has been postulated by [[Evolutionary psychology|evolutionary psychologists]] that moderate versions of compulsive behavior may have had evolutionary advantages. Examples would be moderate constant checking of hygiene, the [[hearth]] or the environment for enemies. Similarly, [[hoarding]] may have had evolutionary advantages. In this view, OCD may be the extreme statistical tail of such behaviors, possibly the result of a high number of predisposing genes.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bracha HS | title = Human brain evolution and the "Neuroevolutionary Time-depth Principle:" Implications for the Reclassification of fear-circuitry-related traits in DSM-V and for studying resilience to warzone-related posttraumatic stress disorder | journal = Progress in Neuro-Psychopharmacology & Biological Psychiatry | volume = 30 | issue = 5 | pages = 827–853 | date = July 2006 | pmid = 16563589 | pmc = 7130737 | doi = 10.1016/j.pnpbp.2006.01.008 }}</ref>

=== Brain structure and functioning ===
[[Medical imaging|Imaging]] studies have shown differences in the [[Frontal lobe|frontal cortex]] and [[Cerebral cortex|subcortical]] structures of the brain in patients with OCD. There appears to be a connection between the OCD symptoms and abnormalities in certain areas of the brain, but such a connection is not clear.<ref name="NIMH">{{Cite web |title=Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder |url=https://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/topics/obsessive-compulsive-disorder-ocd |access-date=23 March 2022 |website=National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) |language=en}}</ref> Some people with OCD have areas of unusually high activity in their brain or low levels of the chemical [[serotonin]],<ref name="nhs.uk_2021b">{{Cite web |date=16 February 2021 |title=Overview - Obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD) |url=https://www.nhs.uk/mental-health/conditions/obsessive-compulsive-disorder-ocd/overview/ |access-date=23 March 2022 |website=nhs.uk |language=en}}</ref> which is a [[neurotransmitter]] that some [[nerve cells]] use to communicate with each other,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Obsessive-compulsive Disorder {{!}} NAMI: National Alliance on Mental Illness |url=https://www.nami.org/About-Mental-Illness/Mental-Health-Conditions/Obsessive-compulsive-Disorder |access-date=23 March 2022 |website=www.nami.org}}</ref> and is thought to be involved in regulating many functions, influencing emotions, mood, memory and sleep.<ref name="How SSRIs work {{!}} OCD-UK">{{Cite web |title=How SSRIs work {{!}} OCD-UK |url=https://www.ocduk.org/overcoming-ocd/medication/how-ssri-work/ |access-date=23 March 2022 |language=en-GB}}</ref>

=== Autoimmune ===
A controversial hypothesis is that some cases of rapid onset of OCD in children and adolescents may be caused by a syndrome connected to [[Group A streptococcal infection]]s (GABHS), known as pediatric autoimmune neuropsychiatric disorders associated with streptococcal infections ([[PANDAS]]).<ref name="Sigra2018">{{cite journal | vauthors = Sigra S, Hesselmark E, Bejerot S | title = Treatment of PANDAS and PANS: a systematic review | journal = Neuroscience and Biobehavioral Reviews | volume = 86 | pages = 51–65 | date = March 2018 | pmid = 29309797 | doi = 10.1016/j.neubiorev.2018.01.001 | doi-access = free | url = http://urn.kb.se/resolve?urn=urn:nbn:se:oru:diva-64031 }}</ref><ref name="Wilbur2019">{{cite journal | vauthors = Wilbur C, Bitnun A, Kronenberg S, Laxer RM, Levy DM, Logan WJ, Shouldice M, Yeh EA | title = PANDAS/PANS in childhood: Controversies and evidence | journal = Paediatrics & Child Health | volume = 24 | issue = 2 | pages = 85–91 | date = May 2019 | pmid = 30996598 | pmc = 6462125 | doi = 10.1093/pch/pxy145 }}</ref><ref name="Boileau2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Boileau B | title = A review of obsessive-compulsive disorder in children and adolescents | journal = Dialogues in Clinical Neuroscience | volume = 13 | issue = 4 | pages = 401–411 | year = 2011 | pmid = 22275846 | pmc = 3263388 | doi = 10.31887/DCNS.2011.13.4/bboileau }}</ref> OCD and tic disorders are hypothesized to arise in a subset of children as a result of a post-[[streptococcus|streptococcal]] autoimmune process.<ref name=
