Dale2017/><ref name= Marazziti2018/><ref name= Zibordi2018/> The PANDAS hypothesis is unconfirmed and unsupported by data and two new categories have been proposed: [[PANDAS|PANS]] (pediatric acute-onset neuropsychiatric syndrome) and CANS (childhood acute neuropsychiatric syndrome).<ref name= Marazziti2018/><ref name= Zibordi2018/> The CANS and PANS hypotheses include different possible mechanisms underlying acute-onset neuropsychiatric conditions, but do not exclude GABHS infections as a cause in a subset of individuals.<ref name= Marazziti2018/><ref name= Zibordi2018/> PANDAS, PANS and CANS are the focus of clinical and laboratory research, but remain unproven.<ref name="Dale2017">{{cite journal | vauthors = Dale RC | title = Tics and Tourette: a clinical, pathophysiological and etiological review | journal = Current Opinion in Pediatrics | volume = 29 | issue = 6 | pages = 665–673 | date = December 2017 | pmid = 28915150 | doi = 10.1097/MOP.0000000000000546 | type = Review }}</ref><ref name="Marazziti2018">{{cite journal | vauthors = Marazziti D, Mucci F, Fontenelle LF | title = Immune system and obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Psychoneuroendocrinology | volume = 93 | pages = 39–44 | date = July 2018 | pmid = 29689421 | doi = 10.1016/j.psyneuen.2018.04.013 | type = Review }}</ref><ref name="Zibordi2018">{{cite journal | vauthors = Zibordi F, Zorzi G, Carecchio M, Nardocci N | title = CANS: Childhood acute neuropsychiatric syndromes | journal = European Journal of Paediatric Neurology | volume = 22 | issue = 2 | pages = 316–320 | date = March 2018 | pmid = 29398245 | doi = 10.1016/j.ejpn.2018.01.011 | type = Review }}</ref> Whether PANDAS is a distinct entity differing from other cases of tic disorders or OCD is debated.<ref name="Shulman2009">{{cite journal | vauthors = Shulman ST | title = Pediatric autoimmune neuropsychiatric disorders associated with streptococci (PANDAS): update | journal = Current Opinion in Pediatrics | volume = 21 | issue = 1 | pages = 127–130 | date = February 2009 | pmid = 19242249 | doi = 10.1097/MOP.0b013e32831db2c4 | quote = Despite continued research in the field, the relationship between GAS and specific neuropsychiatric disorders (PANDAS) remains elusive. }}</ref><ref name="Maia2008">{{cite journal | vauthors = Maia TV, Cooney RE, Peterson BS | title = The neural bases of obsessive-compulsive disorder in children and adults | journal = Development and Psychopathology | volume = 20 | issue = 4 | pages = 1251–1283 | year = 2008 | pmid = 18838041 | pmc = 3079445 | doi = 10.1017/S0954579408000606 }}</ref><ref name="Robertson2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Robertson MM | title = Gilles de la Tourette syndrome: the complexities of phenotype and treatment | journal = British Journal of Hospital Medicine | volume = 72 | issue = 2 | pages = 100–107 | date = February 2011 | pmid = 21378617 | doi = 10.12968/hmed.2011.72.2.100 }}</ref><ref name="Singer2011">{{Cite book | vauthors = Singer HS |title=Hyperkinetic Movement Disorders |year=2011 |isbn=978-0-444-52014-2 |series=Handbook of Clinical Neurology |volume=100 |pages=641–57 |chapter=Tourette syndrome and other tic disorders |doi=10.1016/B978-0-444-52014-2.00046-X |pmid=21496613}}</ref>

A review of studies examining anti-basal ganglia [[Antibody|antibodies]] in OCD found an increased risk of having anti-basal ganglia antibodies in those with OCD versus the general population.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Pearlman DM, Vora HS, Marquis BG, Najjar S, Dudley LA | title = Anti-basal ganglia antibodies in primary obsessive-compulsive disorder: systematic review and meta-analysis | journal = The British Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 205 | issue = 1 | pages = 8–16 | date = July 2014 | pmid = 24986387 | doi = 10.1192/bjp.bp.113.137018 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

=== Environment ===
OCD may be more common in people who have been bullied, abused or neglected, and it sometimes starts after a significant life event, such as childbirth or [[bereavement]].<ref name="nhs.uk_2021b" /> It has been reported in some studies that there is a connection between [[childhood trauma]] and obsessive-compulsive symptoms. More research is needed to understand this relationship better.<ref name="NIMH" />

== Mechanisms ==
{{Main|Biology of obsessive–compulsive disorder}}

=== Neuroimaging ===
[[File:OCD - Obsessive–compulsive disorder.jpg|thumb|upright=1.35|Some parts of the brain showing abnormal activity in OCD: Orbitofrontal cortex integrates rewards, emotions, and behaviors; anterior cingulate cortex is involved in error detection; amygdala is involved in emotional interpretation of reward]]

Functional neuroimaging during symptom provocation has observed abnormal activity in the [[orbitofrontal cortex]] (OFC), left [[dorsolateral prefrontal cortex]] (dlPFC), right [[premotor cortex]], left superior [[Superior temporal gyrus|temporal gyrus]], [[globus pallidus externus]], [[hippocampus]] and right [[uncus]]. Weaker foci of abnormal activity were found in the left [[caudate nucleus|caudate]], [[posterior cingulate cortex]] and [[superior parietal lobule]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Rotge JY, Guehl D, Dilharreguy B, Cuny E, Tignol J, Bioulac B, Allard M, Burbaud P, Aouizerate B | title = Provocation of obsessive-compulsive symptoms: a quantitative voxel-based meta-analysis of functional neuroimaging studies | journal = Journal of Psychiatry & Neuroscience | volume = 33 | issue = 5 | pages = 405–412 | date = September 2008 | doi = 10.1139/jpn.0838 | pmid = 18787662 | pmc = 2527721 }}</ref> However, an older meta-analysis of functional neuroimaging in OCD reported that the only consistent functional neuroimaging finding was increased activity in the [[Orbital gyri|orbital gyrus]] and head of the [[caudate nucleus]], while [[anterior cingulate cortex]] (ACC) activation abnormalities were too inconsistent.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Whiteside SP, Port JD, Abramowitz JS | title = A meta-analysis of functional neuroimaging in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Psychiatry Research | volume = 132 | issue = 1 | pages = 69–79 | date = November 2004 | pmid = 15546704 | doi = 10.1016/j.pscychresns.2004.07.001 }}</ref>

A meta-analysis comparing affective and nonaffective tasks observed differences with controls in regions implicated in [[Salience (neuroscience)|salience]], habit, goal-directed behavior, self-referential thinking and cognitive control. For nonaffective tasks, hyperactivity was observed in the [[Insular cortex|insula]], ACC and head of the [[Caudate nucleus|caudate]]/[[putamen]], while hypoactivity was observed in the [[medial prefrontal cortex]] (mPFC) and posterior caudate. Affective tasks were observed to relate to increased activation in the [[precuneus]] and [[posterior cingulate cortex]], while decreased activation was found in the [[Globus pallidus|pallidum]], ventral anterior thalamus and posterior caudate.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Rasgon A, Lee WH, Leibu E, Laird A, Glahn D, Goodman W, Frangou S | title = Neural correlates of affective and non-affective cognition in obsessive compulsive disorder: A meta-analysis of functional imaging studies | journal = European Psychiatry | volume = 46 | pages = 25–32 | date = October 2017 | pmid = 28992533 | doi = 10.1016/j.eurpsy.2017.08.001 }}</ref> The involvement of the [[Cortico-basal ganglia-thalamo-cortical loop|cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical]] loop in OCD, as well as the high rates of comorbidity between OCD and ADHD, have led some to draw a link in their mechanism. Observed similarities include dysfunction of the [[anterior cingulate cortex]] and [[prefrontal cortex]], as well as shared deficits in executive functions.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Brem S, Grünblatt E, Drechsler R, Riederer P, Walitza S | title = The neurobiological link between OCD and ADHD | journal = Attention Deficit and Hyperactivity Disorders | volume = 6 | issue = 3 | pages = 175–202 | date = September 2014 | pmid = 25017045 | pmc = 4148591 | doi = 10.1007/s12402-014-0146-x }}</ref> The involvement of the orbitofrontal cortex and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex in OCD is shared with [[bipolar disorder]] and may explain the high degree of comorbidity.<ref name="radua2010" /> Decreased volumes of the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex related to executive function has also been observed in OCD.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Piras F, Piras F, Chiapponi C, Girardi P, Caltagirone C, Spalletta G | title = Widespread structural brain changes in OCD: a systematic review of voxel-based morphometry studies | journal = Cortex; A Journal Devoted to the Study of the Nervous System and Behavior | volume = 62 | pages = 89–108 | date = January 2015 | pmid = 23582297 | doi = 10.1016/j.cortex.2013.01.016 }}</ref>

People with OCD evince increased [[grey matter]] volumes in bilateral [[lenticular nucleus|lenticular nuclei]], extending to the caudate nuclei, with decreased grey matter volumes in bilateral dorsal [[medial frontal gyrus|medial frontal]]/[[anterior cingulate cortex|anterior cingulate]] gyri.<ref name="Radua and Mataix-Cols, 2009">{{cite journal | vauthors = Radua J, Mataix-Cols D | title = Voxel-wise meta-analysis of grey matter changes in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = The British Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 195 | issue = 5 | pages = 393–402 | date = November 2009 | pmid = 19880927 | doi = 10.1192/bjp.bp.108.055046 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref name="radua2010">{{cite journal | vauthors = Radua J, van den Heuvel OA, Surguladze S, Mataix-Cols D | title = Meta-analytical comparison of voxel-based morphometry studies in obsessive-compulsive disorder vs other anxiety disorders | journal = Archives of General Psychiatry | volume = 67 | issue = 7 | pages = 701–711 | date = July 2010 | pmid = 20603451 | doi = 10.1001/archgenpsychiatry.2010.70 | doi-access =  }}</ref> These findings contrast with those in people with other anxiety disorders, who evince decreased (rather than increased) grey matter volumes in bilateral lenticular/caudate nuclei, as well as decreased grey matter volumes in bilateral dorsal [[medial frontal gyrus|medial frontal]]/[[anterior cingulate cortex|anterior cingulate]] gyri.<ref name="radua2010" /> Increased [[white matter]] volume and decreased [[fractional anisotropy]] in anterior midline tracts has been observed in OCD, possibly indicating increased fiber crossings.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Radua J, Grau M, van den Heuvel OA, Thiebaut de Schotten M, Stein DJ, Canales-Rodríguez EJ, Catani M, Mataix-Cols D | title = Multimodal voxel-based meta-analysis of white matter abnormalities in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Neuropsychopharmacology | volume = 39 | issue = 7 | pages = 1547–1557 | date = June 2014 | pmid = 24407265 | pmc = 4023155 | doi = 10.1038/npp.2014.5 }}</ref>

=== Cognitive models ===

Generally, two categories of models for OCD have been postulated. The first category involves deficits in executive dysfunction and is based on the observed structural and functional abnormalities in the dlPFC, [[striatum]] and [[thalamus]]. The second category involves dysfunctional [[Modulatory arousal system|modulatory]] control and primarily relies on observed functional and structural differences in the ACC, mPFC and OFC.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Friedlander L, Desrocher M | title = Neuroimaging studies of obsessive-compulsive disorder in adults and children | journal = Clinical Psychology Review | volume = 26 | issue = 1 | pages = 32–49 | date = January 2006 | pmid = 16242823 | doi = 10.1016/j.cpr.2005.06.010 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Stern ER, Taylor SF | title = Cognitive neuroscience of obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = The Psychiatric Clinics of North America | volume = 37 | issue = 3 | pages = 337–352 | date = September 2014 | pmid = 25150566 | doi = 10.1016/j.psc.2014.05.004 }}</ref>

One proposed model suggests that dysfunction in the [[orbitofrontal cortex|orbitalfrontal cortex]] (OFC) leads to improper valuation of behaviors and decreased behavioral control, while the observed alterations in [[amygdala]] activations leads to exaggerated fears and representations of negative stimuli.<ref name="Wood">{{cite journal | vauthors = Wood J, Ahmari SE | title = A Framework for Understanding the Emerging Role of Corticolimbic-Ventral Striatal Networks in OCD-Associated Repetitive Behaviors | journal = Frontiers in Systems Neuroscience | volume = 9 | page = 171 | date = 2015 | pmid = 26733823 | pmc = 4681810 | doi = 10.3389/fnsys.2015.00171 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Due to the [[Homogeneity and heterogeneity|heterogeneity]] of OCD symptoms, studies differentiating various symptoms have been performed. Symptom-specific neuroimaging abnormalities include the hyperactivity of caudate and ACC in checking rituals, while finding increased activity of cortical and [[Cerebellum|cerebellar]] regions in contamination-related symptoms. Neuroimaging differentiating content of intrusive thoughts has found differences between aggressive as opposed to [[taboo]] thoughts, finding increased connectivity of the [[amygdala]], [[ventral striatum]] and [[ventromedial prefrontal cortex]] in aggressive symptoms, while observing increased connectivity between the ventral striatum and insula in sexual or religious intrusive thoughts.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Nakao T, Okada K, Kanba S | title = Neurobiological model of obsessive-compulsive disorder: evidence from recent neuropsychological and neuroimaging findings | journal = Psychiatry and Clinical Neurosciences | volume = 68 | issue = 8 | pages = 587–605 | date = August 2014 | pmid = 24762196 | doi = 10.1111/pcn.12195 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

Another model proposes that affective dysregulation links excessive reliance on habit-based action selection<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Barahona-Corrêa JB, Camacho M, Castro-Rodrigues P, Costa R, Oliveira-Maia AJ | title = From Thought to Action: How the Interplay Between Neuroscience and Phenomenology Changed Our Understanding of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder | journal = Frontiers in Psychology | volume = 6 | page = 1798 | date = 2015 | pmid = 26635696 | pmc = 4655583 | doi = 10.3389/fpsyg.2015.01798 | doi-access = free }}</ref> with compulsions. This is supported by the observation that those with OCD demonstrate decreased activation of the ventral striatum when anticipating monetary reward, as well as increased functional connectivity between the VS and the OFC. Furthermore, those with OCD demonstrate reduced performance in [[Classical conditioning|Pavlovian]] fear-extinction tasks, hyperresponsiveness in the amygdala to fearful stimuli and hyporesponsiveness in the amygdala when exposed to positively valanced stimuli. Stimulation of the [[nucleus accumbens]] has also been observed to effectively alleviate both obsessions and compulsions, supporting the role of affective dysregulation in generating both.<ref name="Wood" />

=== Neurobiological ===

From the observation of the efficacy of antidepressants in OCD, a serotonin hypothesis of OCD has been formulated. Studies of peripheral markers of serotonin, as well as challenges with proserotonergic compounds have yielded inconsistent results, including evidence pointing towards basal hyperactivity of [[Serotonin|serotonergic]] systems.<ref>{{Cite book | vauthors = Davis KL |title=Neuropsychopharmacology: the fifth generation of progress: an official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology |date=2002 |publisher=Lippincott Williams & Wilkins |isbn=978-0-7817-2837-9 |edition=5th |location=Philadelphia, Pa. |pages=1609–1610}}</ref> [[Serotonin receptor]] and transporter binding studies have yielded conflicting results, including higher and lower serotonin receptor [[5-HT2A|5-HT<sub>2A</sub>]] and [[serotonin transporter]] binding potentials that were normalized by treatment with SSRIs. Despite inconsistencies in the types of abnormalities found, evidence points towards dysfunction of serotonergic systems in OCD.<ref>{{Cite book | vauthors = Muller CP, Jacobs BL, Dijk A, Klompmakers A, Deny D |title=Handbook of the behavioral neurobiology of serotonin s |date=2009 |publisher=Academic |isbn=978-0-12-374634-4 |edition=1st |location=London |pages=547–558 |chapter=4.4 The Serotonergic System in Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder}}</ref> Orbitofrontal cortex overactivity is attenuated in people who have successfully responded to SSRI medication, a result believed to be caused by increased stimulation of serotonin receptors [[5-HT2A receptor|5-HT<sub>2A</sub>]] and [[5-HT receptor|5-HT<sub>2C</sub>]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kim KW, Lee DY | title = Obsessive-compulsive disorder associated with a left orbitofrontal infarct | journal = The Journal of Neuropsychiatry and Clinical Neurosciences | volume = 14 | issue = 1 | pages = 88–89 | year = 2002 | pmid = 11884667 | doi = 10.1176/appi.neuropsych.14.1.88 }}</ref>

Dopaminergic dysfunction in cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuits has been implicated in cognitive rigidity, emotional dysregulation, and compulsive behaviors in OCD.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mota |first1=Pedro |last2=Picó-Pérez |first2=Maria |last3=Morgado |first3=Pedro |date=2026 |title=Mapping dopamine's role in obsessive-compulsive disorder: A scoping review of neural circuits, brain regions, and behavioral implications |journal=Journal of Psychiatric Research |volume=194 |pages=11–20 |doi=10.1016/j.jpsychires.2025.12.007 |doi-access=free |pmid=41420895 }}</ref>  A 2020 review found striatal D<sub>2</sub> receptors are decreased in OCD, dopamine transporters are unchanged in OCD, and evidence for other dopaminergic changes is limited.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Dong |first1=Mei-Xue |last2=Chen |first2=Guang-Hui |last3=Hu |first3=Ling |title=Dopaminergic System Alteration in Anxiety and Compulsive Disorders: A Systematic Review of Neuroimaging Studies |journal=Frontiers in Neuroscience |date=3 December 2020 |volume=14 |article-number=608520 |doi=10.3389/fnins.2020.608520 |doi-access=free |pmid=33343291 |pmc=7744599 }}</ref> Increased dopamine release in the [[nucleus accumbens]] after deep brain stimulation correlates with improvement in symptoms, pointing to reduced dopamine release in the striatum playing a role in generating symptoms.<ref>{{Cite book | vauthors = Graat I, Figee M, Denys D |title=Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: Phenomenology, Pathophysiology and Treatment  |publisher=Oxford University Press | veditors = Pittinger C |chapter=Neurotransmitter Dysregulation in OCD}}</ref>

Abnormalities in [[glutamatergic]] [[neurotransmission]] have been implicated in OCD. Findings such as increased [[Cerebrospinal fluid|cerebrospinal]] [[Glutamic acid|glutamate]], less consistent abnormalities observed in neuroimaging studies, and the efficacy of some glutamatergic drugs (such as the glutamate-inhibiting [[riluzole]]) have implicated glutamate in OCD.<ref name="glutamate">{{cite journal | vauthors = Pittenger C, Bloch MH, Williams K | title = Glutamate abnormalities in obsessive compulsive disorder: neurobiology, pathophysiology, and treatment | journal = Pharmacology & Therapeutics | volume = 132 | issue = 3 | pages = 314–332 | date = December 2011 | pmid = 21963369 | pmc = 3205262 | doi = 10.1016/j.pharmthera.2011.09.006 }}</ref> OCD has been associated with reduced [[N-Acetylaspartic acid]] in the mPFC, which is thought to reflect neuron density or functionality, although the exact interpretation has not been established.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Aoki Y, Aoki A, Suwa H | title = Reduction of N-acetylaspartate in the medial prefrontal cortex correlated with symptom severity in obsessive-compulsive disorder: meta-analyses of (1)H-MRS studies | journal = Translational Psychiatry | volume = 2 | issue = 8 | pages = e153 | date = August 2012 | pmid = 22892718 | pmc = 3432192 | doi = 10.1038/tp.2012.78 }}</ref>

== Diagnosis ==

Formal diagnosis may be performed by a [[psychologist]], [[psychiatrist]], clinical [[social work]]er or other licensed mental health professional. OCD, like other mental and behavioral health disorders, cannot be diagnosed by a medical exam,<ref name="Sartorius" /> nor are there any medical exams that can predict if one will fall victim to such illnesses. To be diagnosed with OCD, a person must have obsessions, compulsions or both, according to the [[Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders]] (DSM). The DSM notes that there are multiple characteristics that can turn obsessions and compulsions from normalized behavior to "clinically significant". There has to be recurring and strong thoughts or impulsive that intrude on the day-to-day lives of the patients and cause noticeable levels of anxiousness.<ref name="DSM52" />

These thoughts, impulses or images are of a degree or type that lies outside the [[Normality (behavior)|normal]] range of worries about conventional problems.<ref name="Quick">{{cite book |title=Quick Reference to the Diagnostic Criteria from DSM-IV-TR |date=2000 |publisher=American Psychiatric Association Publishing |isbn=978-0-89042-026-3 }}{{pn|date=February 2026}}</ref> A person may attempt to ignore or [[Thought suppression|suppress]] such obsessions, neutralize them with another thought or action, or try to rationalize their anxiety away. People with OCD tend to recognize their obsessions as irrational.

Compulsions become clinically significant when a person feels driven to perform them in response to an obsession or according to rules that must be applied rigidly and when the person consequently feels or causes significant distress. Therefore, while many people who do not have OCD may perform actions often associated with OCD (such as ordering items in a pantry by height), the distinction with clinically significant OCD lies in the fact that the person with OCD must perform these actions to avoid significant psychological distress. These behaviors or mental acts are aimed at preventing or reducing distress or preventing some dreaded event or situation; however, these activities are not logically or practically connected to the issue or they are excessive.

Moreover, the obsessions or compulsions must be time-consuming, often taking up more than one hour per day or cause impairment in social, occupational or scholastic functioning.<ref name="Quick" /> It is helpful to quantify the severity of symptoms and impairment before and during treatment for OCD. In addition to the person's estimate of the time spent each day harboring obsessive-compulsive thoughts or behaviors, concrete tools can be used to gauge the person's condition. This may be done with rating scales, such as the [[Yale–Brown Obsessive–Compulsive Scale]] (Y-BOCS; expert rating)<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Goodman WK, Price LH, Rasmussen SA, Mazure C, Fleischmann RL, Hill CL, Heninger GR, Charney DS | title = The Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale. I. Development, use, and reliability | journal = Archives of General Psychiatry | volume = 46 | issue = 11 | pages = 1006–1011 | date = November 1989 | pmid = 2684084 | doi = 10.1001/archpsyc.1989.01810110048007 }}</ref> or the obsessive–compulsive inventory (OCI-R; self-rating).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Foa EB, Huppert JD, Leiberg S, Langner R, Kichic R, Hajcak G, Salkovskis PM | title = The Obsessive-Compulsive Inventory: development and validation of a short version | journal = Psychological Assessment | volume = 14 | issue = 4 | pages = 485–496 | date = December 2002 | pmid = 12501574 | doi = 10.1037/1040-3590.14.4.485 }}</ref> A 2025 systematic review found that the 8-question Child Behavior Checklist–Obsessive-Compulsive Subscale (CBCL-OCD/OCS) demonstrates moderate accuracy in identifying children likely to have OCD and may help guide referrals for specialist evaluation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Adam |first1=Gaelen P. |last2=Caputo |first2=Eduardo L. |last3=Kanaan |first3=Ghid |last4=Freeman |first4=Jennifer B. |last5=Brannan |first5=Elizabeth H. |last6=Balk |first6=Ethan M. |last7=Trikalinos |first7=Thomas A. |last8=Steele |first8=Dale W. |date=2025-03-01 |title=Brief Assessment Tools for Obsessive-Compulsive Disorders in Children: A Systematic Review |journal=Pediatrics |language=en |volume=155 |issue=3 |article-number=e2024068993 |doi=10.1542/peds.2024-068993 |pmid=39639450 |doi-access=free }}</ref> With measurements such as these, psychiatric consultation can be more appropriately determined, as it has been standardized.<ref name="Fenske_2009" />

In regards to diagnosing, the health professional also looks to make sure that the signs of obsessions and compulsions are not the results of any drugs, prescription or recreational, that the patient may be taking.<ref name="Cleveland_Clinic">{{Cite web |title=Obsessive Compulsive Disorder (OCD): Symptoms & Treatment |url=https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/9490-obsessive-compulsive-disorder |access-date=12 October 2022 |website=Cleveland Clinic}}</ref>

There are several types of obsessive thoughts that are found commonly in those with OCD. Some of these include fear of germs, hurting loved ones, embarrassment, neatness, societally unacceptable sexual thoughts etc.<ref name="Cleveland_Clinic" /> Within OCD, these specific categories are often diagnosed into their own type of OCD.<ref name="DSM52" /> OCD is sometimes placed in a group of disorders called the [[obsessive–compulsive spectrum]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Starcevic V, Janca A | title = Obsessive-compulsive spectrum disorders: still in search of the concept-affirming boundaries | journal = Current Opinion in Psychiatry | volume = 24 | issue = 1 | pages = 55–60 | date = January 2011 | pmid = 20827198 | doi = 10.1097/yco.0b013e32833f3b58 }}</ref>

Another criterion in the DSM is that a person's mental illness does not fit one of the other categories of a mental disorder better. That is to say, if the obsessions and compulsions of a patient could be better described by [[trichotillomania]], it would not be diagnosed as OCD.<ref name="DSM52" /> That being said, OCD does often go hand in hand with other mental disorders. For this reason, one may be diagnosed with multiple mental disorders at once.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Freckelton I | title = Obsessive compulsive disorder and obsessive compulsive personality disorder and the criminal law | journal = Psychiatry, Psychology and Law | volume = 27 | issue = 5 | pages = 831–852 | date = June 2020 | pmid = 33833612 | pmc = 8009125 | doi = 10.1080/13218719.2020.1745497 }}</ref>

A different aspect of the diagnoses is the degree of insight had by the individual in regards to the truth of the obsessions. There are three levels, good/fair, poor and absent/delusional. Good/fair indicated that the patient is aware that the obsessions they have are not true or probably not true.<ref name="DSM52" /> Poor indicates that the patient believes their obsessional beliefs are probably true.<ref name="DSM52" /> Absent/delusional indicates that they fully believe their obsessional thoughts to be true.<ref name="DSM52" /> Approximately 4% or fewer individuals with OCD will be diagnosed as absent/delusional.<ref name="DSM52" /> Additionally, as many as 30% of those with OCD also have a lifetime tic disorder, meaning they have been diagnosed with a tic disorder at some point in their life.<ref name="DSM52" /> There are several different types of tics that have been observed in individuals with OCD. These include but are not limited to, "grunting", "jerking" or "shrugging" body parts, sniffling and excessive blinking.<ref name="Cleveland_Clinic" />

There has been a significant amount of progress over the last few decades and, {{as of|2022|lc=yes}}, there is statically significant improvement in the diagnostic process for individuals with OCD. One study found that of two groups of individuals, one with participants under the age of 27.25 and one with participants over that age, those in the younger group experienced a significantly faster time between the onset of OCD tendencies and their formal diagnoses.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Hezel D |date=January 2022 |title=Delay to diagnosis in OCD |journal=Journal of Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders |volume=32 |article-number=100709 |doi=10.1016/j.jocrd.2022.100709 }}</ref>

=== Differential diagnosis ===
OCD is often confused with the separate condition [[obsessive–compulsive personality disorder]] (OCPD). OCD is [[Egosyntonic and egodystonic|egodystonic]], meaning that the disorder is incompatible with the individual's [[self-concept]].<ref name="Aardema, F. 2007" /><ref name="Aardema">{{Cite journal | vauthors = Aardema F, O'Connor K |year=2003 |title=Seeing white bears that are not there: Inference processes in obsessions |journal=Journal of Cognitive Psychotherapy |volume=17 |pages=23–37 |doi= 10.1891/jcop.17.1.23.58270 }}</ref> As egodystonic disorders go against a person's self-concept, they tend to cause much distress. OCPD, on the other hand, is [[Egosyntonic and egodystonic|egosyntonic]], marked by the person's acceptance that the characteristics and behaviors displayed as a result are compatible with their [[self-image]], or are otherwise appropriate, correct or reasonable.

As a result, people with OCD are often aware that their behavior is not rational and are unhappy about their obsessions, but nevertheless feel compelled by them.<ref name="carter">Carter, K. "Obsessive–compulsive personality disorder." PSYC 210 lecture: Oxford College of Emory University. Oxford, GA. 11 April 2006.</ref> By contrast, people with OCPD are not aware of anything abnormal; they will readily explain why their actions are rational. It is usually impossible to convince them otherwise and they tend to derive pleasure from their obsessions or compulsions.<ref name="carter" />

== Management ==
[[Cognitive behavioral therapy]] (CBT) and [[psychotropic medication]]s are the [[first-line treatment]]s for OCD.<ref name="NIH20152" /><ref name="NICE2005" />

=== Therapy ===
In [[cognitive behavioral therapy]] (CBT), OCD patients are asked to overcome intrusive thoughts by not indulging in any compulsions. They are taught that rituals keep OCD strong, while not performing them causes OCD to become weaker.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Understanding CBT for OCD |url=https://www.med.upenn.edu/ctsa/forms_ocd_cbt.html |access-date=31 August 2021 |publisher=[[Perelman School of Medicine at the University of Pennsylvania|Perelman School of Medicine University of Pennsylvania]]}}</ref> This position is supported by the pattern of memory distrust; the more often compulsions are repeated, the more weakened memory trust becomes and this cycle continues as memory distrust increases compulsion frequency.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=van den Hout M, Kindt M |date=June 2004 |title=Obsessive-compulsive disorder and the paradoxical effects of perseverative behaviour on experienced uncertainty |journal=Journal of Behavior Therapy and Experimental Psychiatry |volume=35 |issue=2 |pages=165–181 |doi=10.1016/j.jbtep.2004.04.007 |pmid=15210377 |hdl-access=free |hdl=1874/11267}}</ref> [[File:Pennsylvania Capitol front door keyhole.jpg|thumb|One exposure and ritual prevention activity would be to check the lock only once and then leave.]]

One specific CBT technique used is called [[exposure and response prevention]] (ERP), which involves teaching the person to deliberately come into contact with situations that trigger obsessive thoughts and fears (exposure), without carrying out the usual compulsive acts associated with the obsession (response prevention). This technique causes patients to gradually learn to tolerate the discomfort and anxiety associated with not performing their compulsions. For many patients, ERP is the add-on treatment of choice when [[selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor]]s (SSRIs) medication does not effectively treat OCD symptoms, or vice versa, for individuals who begin treatment with psychotherapy.<ref name="NIMH" /> This technique is considered superior to others due to the lack of medication used. However, up to 25% of patients will discontinue treatment due to the severity of their tics. CBT normally lasts anywhere from 12–16 sessions, with [[Cognitive behavioral training|homework]] assigned to the patient in between meetings with a therapist.<ref name="m564"/> Modalities differ in ERP treatment but both virtual reality based as well as unguided computer assisted treatment programs have shown effective results in treatment programs.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Imai H, Tajika A, Narita H, Yoshinaga N, Kimura K, Nakamura H, Takeshima N, Hayasaka Y, Ogawa Y, Furukawa T | title = Unguided Computer-Assisted Self-Help Interventions Without Human Contact in Patients With Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: Systematic Review and Meta-analysis | journal = Journal of Medical Internet Research | volume = 24 | issue = 4 | article-number = e35940 | date = April 2022 | pmid = 35451993 | pmc = 9073609 | doi = 10.2196/35940 | doi-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = van Loenen I, Scholten W, Muntingh A, Smit J, Batelaan N | title = The Effectiveness of Virtual Reality Exposure-Based Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Severe Anxiety Disorders, Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder, and Posttraumatic Stress Disorder: Meta-analysis | journal = Journal of Medical Internet Research | volume = 24 | issue = 2 | article-number = e26736 | date = February 2022 | pmid = 35142632 | pmc = 8874794 | doi = 10.2196/26736 | doi-access = free }}</ref> A 2024 systematic review concluded, with high strength of evidence, that CBT with ERP provided via telehealth is equally as effective for reducing symptoms, in children and adolescents, as in-person treatment.<ref name="Steele 2024" />

For example, a patient might be asked to touch something very mildly contaminated (exposure) and wash their hands only once afterward (response prevention). Another example might entail asking the patient to leave the house and check the lock only once (exposure), without going back to check again (response prevention). After succeeding at one stage of treatment, the patient's level of discomfort in the exposure phase can be increased. When this therapy is successful, the patient will quickly [[Habituation|habituate]] to an anxiety-producing situation, discovering a considerable drop in anxiety level.<ref name="baojournal.com">Huppert & Roth: (2003) Treating Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder with Exposure and Response Prevention. ''The Behavior Analyst Today, 4 (1),'' 66 – 70 [http://www.baojournal.com/ BAO] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100323025324/http://baojournal.com/ |date=23 March 2010 }}</ref>

ERP has a strong evidence base and is considered the most effective treatment for OCD.<ref name="baojournal.com" /> However, this claim was doubted by some researchers in 2000, who criticized the quality of many studies.<ref name="pmid10910778">{{cite journal | vauthors = Klein DF | title = Flawed meta-analyses comparing psychotherapy with pharmacotherapy | journal = The American Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 157 | issue = 8 | pages = 1204–1211 | date = August 2000 | pmid = 10910778 | doi = 10.1176/appi.ajp.157.8.1204 }}</ref> While ERP can lead a majority of clients to improvements, many do not reach remission or become asymptomatic;<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fisher PL, Cherry MG, Stuart T, Rigby JW, Temple J | title = People with obsessive-compulsive disorder often remain symptomatic following psychological treatment: A clinical significance analysis of manualised psychological interventions | journal = Journal of Affective Disorders | volume = 275 | pages = 94–108 | date = October 2020 | pmid = 32658831 | doi = 10.1016/j.jad.2020.06.019 }}</ref> some therapists are also hesitant to use this approach.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Schneider SC, Knott L, Cepeda SL, Hana LM, McIngvale E, Goodman WK, Storch EA | title = Serious negative consequences associated with exposure and response prevention for obsessive-compulsive disorder: A survey of therapist attitudes and experiences | journal = Depression and Anxiety | volume = 37 | issue = 5 | pages = 418–428 | date = May 2020 | pmid = 32048376 | doi = 10.1002/da.23000 | doi-access =  }}</ref>

The recent development of remotely technology-delivered CBT is increasing access to therapy options for those living with OCD and remote versions appear to be equally as effective as in-person therapy options. The development of smartphone interventions for OCD that utilize CBT techniques are another alternative that is expanding access to therapy while allowing therapies to be personalized for each patient.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Jalal B, Chamberlain SR, Robbins TW, Sahakian BJ | title = Obsessive-compulsive disorder-contamination fears, features, and treatment: novel smartphone therapies in light of global mental health and pandemics (COVID-19) | journal = CNS Spectrums | volume = 27 | issue = 2 | pages = 136–144 | date = April 2022 | pmid = 33081864 | pmc = 7691644 | doi = 10.1017/S1092852920001947 }}</ref>

[[Acceptance and commitment therapy]] (ACT), a newer therapy also used to treat anxiety and depression, has also been found to be effective in treatment of OCD.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Twohig MP, Hayes SC, Masuda A | title = Increasing willingness to experience obsessions: acceptance and commitment therapy as a treatment for obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Behavior Therapy | volume = 37 | issue = 1 | pages = 3–13 | date = March 2006 | pmid = 16942956 | doi = 10.1016/j.beth.2005.02.001 | url = https://scholarworks.gsu.edu/psych_facpub/100 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Twohig MP, Abramowitz JS, Bluett EJ, Fabricant LE, Jacoby RJ, Morrison KL, Reuman L, Smith BM  |date=1 July 2015 |title=Exposure therapy for OCD from an acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT) framework |journal=Journal of Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders |volume=6 |pages=167–173 |doi=10.1016/j.jocrd.2014.12.007 }}</ref> ACT uses [[acceptance]] and [[mindfulness]] strategies to teach patients not to overreact to or avoid unpleasant thoughts and feelings but rather "move toward valued behavior".<ref>{{Cite web | vauthors = Hayes SC |title=ACT {{!}} Association for Contextual Behavioral Science |url=https://contextualscience.org/act |access-date=7 July 2022 |website=contextualscience.org}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book | vauthors = Hayes SC, Strosahl K, Wilson KG |title=Acceptance and commitment therapy: the process and practice of mindful change |date=2012 |publisher=Guilford Press |isbn=978-1-60918-962-4 |edition=2nd |location=New York |oclc=713181786}}</ref>

[[Inference-based therapy]] (IBT) is a form of [[cognitive therapy]] specifically developed for treating OCD.<ref name="O'Connor, K. 2005">O'Connor, K., Aardema, F., & Pelissier, M.-C. (2005). Beyond reasonable doubt: Reasoning processes in obsessive-compulsive disorder and related disorders. Chichester: John Wiley & Sons.</ref> The therapy posits that individuals with OCD put a greater emphasis on an imagined possibility than on what can be perceived with the [[sense]]s, and confuse the imagined possibility with reality, in a process called [[inferential confusion]].<ref>Aardema, F., O'Connor, K. P., Emmelkamp, P. M., Marchand, A., & Todorov, C. (2005). Inferential confusion in obsessive-compulsive disorder: the inferential confusion questionnaire. Behaviour Research & Therapy, 43, 293-308.</ref> According to inference-based therapy, obsessional thinking occurs when the person replaces reality and real probabilities with imagined possibilities.<ref>O'Connor, K. (2002). Intrusions and inferences in obsessive compulsive disorder. Clinical Psychology & Psychotherapy, 9, 38-46.</ref> The goal of inference-based therapy is to reorient clients towards trusting the senses and relating to reality in a normal, non-effortful way. Differences between normal and obsessional doubts are presented and clients are encouraged to use their senses and reasoning as they do in non-obsessive–compulsive disorder situations.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = O'Connor K, Aardema F | title = Clinician's handbook for obsessive compulsive disorder: Inference-based therapy. | location = Chichester | publisher = Wiley-Blackwell | date = 2011 }}</ref><ref name="O'Connor, K. 2005"/> Research on Inference-Based Cognitive-Behavior Therapy (I-CBT) suggests it can lead to improvements for those with OCD.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Aardema F, Bouchard S, Koszycki D, Lavoie ME, Audet JS, O'Connor K | title = Evaluation of Inference-Based Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy for Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Multicenter Randomized Controlled Trial with Three Treatment Modalities | journal = Psychotherapy and Psychosomatics | volume = 91 | issue = 5 | pages = 348–359 | date = 2022 | pmid = 35584639 | doi = 10.1159/000524425 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

For [[body-focused repetitive behavior]]s such as [[trichotillomania]] (hair pulling), [[Excoriation disorder|skin picking]] and [[Nail biting|onychophagia]] (nail biting), behavioral interventions such as [[habit reversal training]] and [[Decoupling for body-focused repetitive behaviors|decoupling]] are recommended for the treatment of compulsive behaviors.<ref name="Himle_2004">{{Cite journal |vauthors=Himle MB, Flessner CA, Woods DW |date=2004 |title=Advances in the behavior analytic treatment of trichotillomania and Tourette's Syndrome |journal=Journal of Early and Intensive Behavior Intervention |volume=1 |issue=1 |pages=57–64 |doi=10.1037/h0100282 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Sarris_2012">{{cite journal |vauthors=Sarris J, Camfield D, Berk M |date=May 2012 |title=Complementary medicine, self-help, and lifestyle interventions for obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD) and the OCD spectrum: a systematic review |journal=Journal of Affective Disorders |volume=138 |issue=3 |pages=213–221 |doi=10.1016/j.jad.2011.04.051 |pmid=21620478}}</ref>

A 2007 [[Cochrane (organisation)|Cochrane review]] found that psychological interventions derived from CBT models, such as ERP, ACT and IBT, were more effective than non-CBT interventions.<ref name="Gava_2007">{{cite journal | vauthors = Gava I, Barbui C, Aguglia E, Carlino D, Churchill R, De Vanna M, McGuire HF | title = Psychological treatments versus treatment as usual for obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD) | journal = The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews | issue = 2 | article-number = CD005333 | date = April 2007 | pmid = 17443583 | doi = 10.1002/14651858.CD005333.pub2 }}</ref> Other forms of psychotherapy, such as [[psychodynamics]] and [[psychoanalysis]], may help in managing some aspects of the disorder. However, in 2007, the [[American Psychiatric Association]] (APA) noted a lack of [[Scientific control|controlled studies]] showing their efficacy, "in dealing with the core symptoms of OCD".<ref name="APAguidelines">{{cite journal | vauthors = Koran LM, Hanna GL, Hollander E, Nestadt G, Simpson HB | title = Practice guideline for the treatment of patients with obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = [[The American Journal of Psychiatry]] | volume = 164 | issue = 7 Suppl | pages = 5–53 | date = July 2007 | pmid = 17849776 }}</ref> For [[body-focused repetitive behavior]]s, behavioral interventions such as [[habit reversal training|habit-reversal training]] and [[Decoupling for body-focused repetitive behaviors|decoupling]] are recommended.<ref name="Himle_2004" /><ref name="Sarris_2012" />

Psychotherapy in combination with psychiatric medication may be more effective than either option alone for individuals with severe OCD.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Skapinakis P, Caldwell DM, Hollingworth W, Bryden P, Fineberg NA, Salkovskis P, Welton NJ, Baxter H, Kessler D, Churchill R, Lewis G | title = Pharmacological and psychotherapeutic interventions for management of obsessive-compulsive disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis | journal = The Lancet. Psychiatry | volume = 3 | issue = 8 | pages = 730–739 | date = August 2016 | pmid = 27318812 | pmc = 4967667 | doi = 10.1016/S2215-0366(16)30069-4 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wheaton MG, DeSantis SM, Simpson HB | title = Network meta-analyses and treatment recommendations for obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = The Lancet. Psychiatry | volume = 3 | issue = 10 | page = 920 | date = October 2016 | pmid = 27692263 | pmc = 6690590 | doi = 10.1016/S2215-0366(16)30280-2 }}</ref><ref name="Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: Adva"/> ERP coupled with weight restoration and serotonin reuptake inhibitors has proven the most effective when treating OCD and an eating disorder simultaneously.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Simpson HB, Wetterneck CT, Cahill SP, Steinglass JE, Franklin ME, Leonard RC, Weltzin TE, Riemann BC | title = Treatment of obsessive-compulsive disorder complicated by comorbid eating disorders | journal = Cognitive Behaviour Therapy | volume = 42 | issue = 1 | pages = 64–76 | date = 1 March 2013 | pmid = 23316878 | pmc = 3947513 | doi = 10.1080/16506073.2012.751124 }}</ref>

=== Medication ===
{{See also|List of investigational obsessive–compulsive disorder drugs}}
[[File:JZoloft JPN.jpg|thumb|A blister pack of sertraline under the brand name Zoloft]]

The medications most frequently used to treat OCD are antidepressants, including [[selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor]]s (SSRIs).<ref name="NEJM2014" /> SSRIs such as [[sertraline]] and [[fluoxetine]] are effective in treating OCD for children and adolescents.<ref>{{cite report |title=Antidepressants for children and teenagers: What works for anxiety and depression? |date=2022 |doi=10.3310/nihrevidence_53342 }}</ref><ref name="Boaden_2020">{{cite journal |vauthors=Boaden K, Tomlinson A, Cortese S, Cipriani A |date=2 September 2020 |title=Antidepressants in Children and Adolescents: Meta-Review of Efficacy, Tolerability and Suicidality in Acute Treatment |journal=Frontiers in Psychiatry |volume=11 |article-number=717 |doi=10.3389/fpsyt.2020.00717 |pmc=7493620 |pmid=32982805|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Correll_2021">{{cite journal |vauthors=Correll CU, Cortese S, Croatto G, Monaco F, Krinitski D, Arrondo G, Ostinelli EG, Zangani C, Fornaro M, Estradé A, Fusar-Poli P, Carvalho AF, Solmi M |date=June 2021 |title=Efficacy and acceptability of pharmacological, psychosocial, and brain stimulation interventions in children and adolescents with mental disorders: an umbrella review |journal=World Psychiatry |volume=20 |issue=2 |pages=244–275 |doi=10.1002/wps.20881 |pmc=8129843 |pmid=34002501}}</ref> However, ERP alone may be as effective as ERP combined with an SSRI for OCD symptoms.<ref name="Steele 2024" /> A 2025 network meta-analysis of 71 randomized trials found that combining exposure therapy with an SSRI was more helpful for children with OCD than taking an SSRI by itself.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Steele |first=Dale W. |last2=Kanaan |first2=Ghid |last3=Caputo |first3=Eduardo L. |last4=Freeman |first4=Jennifer B. |last5=Brannan |first5=Elizabeth H. |last6=Balk |first6=Ethan M. |last7=Trikalinos |first7=Thomas A. |last8=Adam |first8=Gaelen P. |date=2025-03-01 |title=Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder in Children and Youth: A Meta-Analysis |url=https://publications.aap.org/pediatrics/article/155/3/e2024068992/200215/Treatment-of-Obsessive-Compulsive-Disorder-in |journal=Pediatrics |language=en |volume=155 |issue=3 |doi=10.1542/peds.2024-068992 |issn=0031-4005}}</ref>

SSRIs are a second-line treatment of adult OCD with mild functional impairment and as first-line treatment for those with moderate or severe impairment. In children, SSRIs can be considered as a second-line therapy in those with moderate to severe impairment, with close monitoring for psychiatric adverse effects.<ref name="NICE2005">{{Cite web |last=National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence (NICE) |date=November 2005 |title=Obsessive-compulsive disorder: Core interventions in the treatment of obsessive-compulsive disorder and body dysmorphic disorder |url=https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/cg31/resources/treating-obsessivecompulsive-disorder-ocd-and-body-dysmorphic-disorder-bdd-in-adults-children-and-young-people-194882077 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170112001813/https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/cg31/resources/treating-obsessivecompulsive-disorder-ocd-and-body-dysmorphic-disorder-bdd-in-adults-children-and-young-people-194882077 |archive-date=12 January 2017 |access-date=24 July 2016 |series=Information about NICE Clinical Guideline 31 |publisher=UK National Health Service (NHS)}}</ref> Patients treated with SSRIs are about twice as likely to respond to treatment as are those treated with [[placebo]], so this treatment is qualified as efficacious.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Arroll B, Elley CR, Fishman T, Goodyear-Smith FA, Kenealy T, Blashki G, Kerse N, Macgillivray S | title = Antidepressants versus placebo for depression in primary care | journal = The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews | volume = 2009 | issue = 3 | article-number = CD007954 | date = July 2009 | pmid = 19588448 | doi = 10.1002/14651858.CD007954 | pmc = 10576545 | veditors = Arroll B }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Review Finds SSRIs Modestly Effective in Short-Term Treatment of OCD |url=http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/570825+ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130413110435/http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/570825 |archive-date=13 April 2013}}</ref> Efficacy has been demonstrated both in short-term (6–24 weeks) treatment trials and in discontinuation trials with durations of 28–52 weeks.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fineberg NA, Brown A, Reghunandanan S, Pampaloni I | title = Evidence-based pharmacotherapy of obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = The International Journal of Neuropsychopharmacology | volume = 15 | issue = 8 | pages = 1173–1191 | date = September 2012 | pmid = 22226028 | doi = 10.1017/S1461145711001829 | doi-access = free | hdl = 2299/216 | hdl-access = free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Sertraline prescribing information |url=http://www.accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/label/2009/019839s070,020990s032lbl.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150616011817/http://www.accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/label/2009/019839s070%2C020990s032lbl.pdf |archive-date=16 June 2015 |access-date=30 January 2015}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Paroxetine prescribing information |url=https://www.apotex.com/us/en/products/downloads/pil/paxil_irtb_ins.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150219055046/https://www.apotex.com/us/en/products/downloads/pil/paxil_irtb_ins.pdf |archive-date=19 February 2015 |access-date=30 January 2015}}</ref>

[[Clomipramine]], a medication belonging to the class of [[tricyclic antidepressant]]s, appears to work as well as SSRIs, but has a higher rate of side effects.<ref name="NEJM2014" /> [[Clomipramine]] has been shown to be possibly more effective than a placebo.<ref name="Steele 2024" />

In 2006, the [[National Institute for Health and Care Excellence]] (NICE) guidelines recommended augmentative second-generation (atypical) [[antipsychotic]]s for treatment-resistant OCD.<ref name="Ve2014" /> Current evidence is limited regarding their use as monotherapy in OCD.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Crapanzano |first1=Calogero |last2=Francesco Laurenzi |first2=Pier |last3=Casolaro |first3=Ilaria |last4=Politano |first4=Andrea |last5=Amendola |first5=Chiara |title=Antipsychotic Monotherapy in Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder |journal=Psychiatria Danubina |date=2022 |volume=34 |issue=1 |page=106 |url=http://www.psychiatria-danubina.com/UserDocsImages/pdf/dnb_vol34_no1/dnb_vol34_no1_106.pdf |pmid=35467624 }}</ref> For OCD treatment, there is tentative short-term evidence supporting [[risperidone]] and [[aripiprazole]], insufficient evidence for [[olanzapine]], and [[quetiapine]] shows no significant benefit versus placebo, with overall conclusions limited by a small number of studies.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Komossa K, Depping AM, Meyer M, Kissling W, Leucht S | title = Second-generation antipsychotics for obsessive compulsive disorder | journal = The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews | issue = 12 | article-number = CD008141 | date = December 2010 | pmid = 21154394 | doi = 10.1002/14651858.CD008141.pub2 }}</ref><ref name="Ve2014" />

=== Procedures ===
The United States Food and Drug Administration approved transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) as a new treatment for treatment-resistant OCD in 2018.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-04-11 |title=FDA permits marketing of transcranial magnetic stimulation for treatment of obsessive compulsive disorder |url=https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-permits-marketing-transcranial-magnetic-stimulation-treatment-obsessive-compulsive-disorder |access-date=2025-09-24 |website=FDA |language=en}}</ref> Existing evidence suggests that repetitive TMS (rTMS), particularly targeting the [[dorsolateral prefrontal cortex]] and [[supplementary motor area]], effectively reduces obsessive–compulsive disorder symptoms.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Kar |first1=Sujita Kumar |last2=Agrawal |first2=Aditya |last3=Silva-Dos-Santos |first3=Amílcar |last4=Gupta |first4=Yogesh |last5=Deng |first5=Zhi-De |date=2024 |title=The Efficacy of Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation in the Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: An Umbrella Review of Meta-Analyses |journal=CNS Spectrums |volume=29 |issue=2 |pages=109–118 |doi=10.1017/S1092852923006387 |pmc=11524532 |pmid=38053347}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Perera MP, Mallawaarachchi S, Miljevic A, Bailey NW, Herring SE, Fitzgerald PB | title = Repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation for Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Meta-analysis of Randomized, Sham-Controlled Trials | journal = Biological Psychiatry. Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging | volume = 6 | issue = 10 | pages = 947–960 | date = October 2021 | pmid = 33775927 | doi = 10.1016/j.bpsc.2021.03.010 }}</ref>

The evidence is insufficient to conclude that [[electroconvulsive therapy]] is an effective treatment for OCD.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Fontenelle |first=Leonardo F. |last2=Coutinho |first2=Evandro S. F. |last3=Lins-Martins |first3=Natália M. |last4=Fitzgerald |first4=Paul B. |last5=Fujiwara |first5=Hironobo |last6=Yücel |first6=Murat |date=2015 |title=Electroconvulsive therapy for obsessive-compulsive disorder: a systematic review |journal=The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry |volume=76 |issue=7 |pages=949–957 |doi=10.4088/JCP.14r09129 |issn=1555-2101 |pmid=25844888}}</ref> Evidence is limited to low-quality [[Case report|case reports]]/series that suggest possible benefit in some severe, treatment-resistant cases; however, no [[Randomized controlled trial|randomized trials]] have been performed, leaving its efficacy unproven.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Dos Santos-Ribeiro |first=Samara |last2=de Salles Andrade |first2=Juliana B |last3=Quintas |first3=Julliana N |last4=Baptista |first4=Karina B |last5=Moreira-de-Oliveira |first5=Maria E |last6=Yücel |first6=Murat |last7=Fontenelle |first7=Leonardo F |date=2018-10-01 |title=A Systematic Review of the Utility of Electroconvulsive Therapy in Broadly Defined Obsessive-Compulsive-Related Disorders |journal=The primary care companion for CNS disorders |volume=20 |issue=5 |article-number=18r02342 |doi=10.4088/pcc.18r02342 |issn=2155-7780 |pmid=30407758}}</ref>

[[psychosurgery|Surgery]] may be used as a last resort in people who do not improve with other treatments. In this procedure, a surgical [[lesion]] is made in an area of the brain (the [[cingulate cortex]]). In one study, 30% of participants benefitted significantly from this procedure.<ref name="Barlow">Barlow, D. H. and V. M. Durand. ''Essentials of Abnormal Psychology''. California: Thomson Wadsworth, 2006.</ref> [[Deep brain stimulation]] and [[vagus nerve stimulation]] are possible surgical options that do not require destruction of [[brain tissue]]. However, because deep brain stimulation results in such an instant and intense change, individuals may experience identity challenges afterward.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Bosanac P, Hamilton BE, Lucak J, Castle D | title = Identity challenges and 'burden of normality' after DBS for severe OCD: a narrative case study | journal = BMC Psychiatry | volume = 18 | issue = 1 | article-number = 186 | date = June 2018 | pmid = 29895269 | pmc = 5998583 | doi = 10.1186/s12888-018-1771-2 | doi-access = free }}</ref> In the United States, the [[Food and Drug Administration]] approved deep brain stimulation for the treatment of OCD under a [[Humanitarian Device Exemption|humanitarian device exemption]], requiring that the procedure be performed only in a hospital with special qualifications to do so.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Barlas S |date=8 April 2009 |title=FDA Approves Pioneering Treatment for Obsessive- Compulsive Disorder |url=http://www.psychiatrictimes.com/ocd/article/10168/1399208 |url-status=live |journal=Psychiatric Times |volume=26 |issue=4 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090710101738/http://www.psychiatrictimes.com/ocd/article/10168/1399208 |archive-date=10 July 2009}}</ref>

In the United States, [[psychosurgery]] for OCD is a treatment of last resort and will not be performed until the person has failed several attempts at medication (at the full dosage) with augmentation, and many months of intensive [[cognitive–behavioral therapy|cognitive behavioral therapy]] with exposure and ritual/response prevention.<ref>[http://www.brainphysics.com/surgery.php Surgical Procedures for Obsessive–Compulsive Disorder] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080725094926/http://brainphysics.com/surgery.php |date=25 July 2008 }}, by M. Jahn and M. Williams, PhD,. BrainPhysics OCD Resource, Accessed 6 July 2008.</ref> Likewise, in the United Kingdom, psychosurgery cannot be performed unless a course of treatment from a suitably qualified cognitive–behavioral therapist has been carried out.

=== Children ===
Therapeutic treatment may be effective in reducing ritual behaviors of OCD for children and adolescents.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = O'Donohue W, Ferguson KE |year=2006 |title=Evidence-Based Practice in Psychology and Behavior Analysis |journal=The Behavior Analyst Today |volume=7 |issue=3 |pages=335–347 |doi=10.1037/h0100155|doi-access=free }}</ref> Similar to the treatment of adults with OCD, cognitive behavioral therapy, along with [[Exposure therapy#Exposure and response prevention (ERP)|exposure and response prevention (ERP) therapy]], stands as an effective and validated first line of treatment of OCD in children.<ref name="Freeman2014">{{cite journal | vauthors = Freeman J, Garcia A, Frank H, Benito K, Conelea C, Walther M, Edmunds J | title = Evidence base update for psychosocial treatments for pediatric obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Journal of Clinical Child and Adolescent Psychology | volume = 43 | issue = 1 | pages = 7–26 | date = 2014 | pmid = 23746138 | pmc = 3815743 | doi = 10.1080/15374416.2013.804386 }}</ref><ref name="Steele 2024" /> Family involvement, in the form of behavioral observations and reports, is a key component to the success of such treatments.<ref name="Rapoport, J. E. 1989">{{cite book | vauthors = Rapoport JE | date = 1989 | title = Obsessive-compulsive Disorder in Children & Adolescents. | location = Washington | publisher = American Psychiatric Press }}</ref> Parental interventions also provide positive reinforcement for a child who exhibits appropriate behaviors as alternatives to compulsive responses. In a recent meta-analysis of evidenced-based treatment of OCD in children, family-focused individual CBT was labeled as "probably efficacious", establishing it as one of the leading psychosocial treatments for youth with OCD.<ref name="Freeman2014" /> After one or two years of therapy, in which a child learns the nature of their obsession and acquires strategies for coping, they may acquire a larger circle of friends, exhibit less shyness and become less self-critical.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Adams PL | date = 1973 | title = Obsessive Children: A Sociopsychiatric Study. | location = Philadelphia | publisher = Brunner / Mazel }}</ref> Trials have shown that children and adolescents with OCD should begin treatment with the combination of CBT with a selective [[serotonin reuptake inhibitor]] or CBT alone, rather than only an SSRI.<ref name="Cognitive-Behavior Therapy, Sertral"/><ref name="Practice Parameter for the Assessme"/> A 2024 systematic review of the literature found that combining ERP therapy with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors can enhance treatment outcomes compared to using SSRIs alone.<ref name="Steele 2024" /> ERP therapy can be done in-office or via telehealth since there was no statistically significant difference in effectiveness as shown in the AHRQ study.<ref name="Steele 2024" /> A 2025 meta-analysis found that exposure and response prevention and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are the most commonly studied treatments, with more limited evidence for newer approaches such as antipsychotic augmentation and neuromodulation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Steele |first=Dale W. |last2=Kanaan |first2=Ghid |last3=Caputo |first3=Eduardo L. |last4=Freeman |first4=Jennifer B. |last5=Brannan |first5=Elizabeth H. |last6=Balk |first6=Ethan M. |last7=Trikalinos |first7=Thomas A. |last8=Adam |first8=Gaelen P. |date=2025-03-01 |title=Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder in Children and Youth: A Meta-Analysis |url=https://publications.aap.org/pediatrics/article/155/3/e2024068992/200215/Treatment-of-Obsessive-Compulsive-Disorder-in |journal=Pediatrics |language=en |volume=155 |issue=3 |doi=10.1542/peds.2024-068992 |issn=0031-4005|doi-access=free }}</ref>

Although the known causes of OCD in younger age groups range from brain abnormalities to psychological preoccupations, life stress such as bullying and traumatic familial deaths may also contribute to childhood cases of OCD, and acknowledging these stressors can play a role in treating the disorder.<ref name="pmid19378573">{{cite journal | vauthors = D'Alessandro TM | title = Factors influencing the onset of childhood obsessive compulsive disorder | journal = Pediatric Nursing | volume = 35 | issue = 1 | pages = 43–46 | year = 2009 | pmid = 19378573 }}</ref>

== Prognosis ==
[[Quality of life]] is reduced across all domains in OCD, which is vastly chronic.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Stein |first1=Dan J. |last2=Ruscio |first2=Ayelet Meron |last3=Altwaijri |first3=Yasmin |last4=Chiu |first4=Wai Tat |last5=Sampson |first5=Nancy A. |last6=Aguilar-Gaxiola |first6=Sergio |last7=Al-Hamzawi |first7=Ali |last8=Alonso |first8=Jordi |last9=Chardoul |first9=Stephanie |last10=Gureje |first10=Oye |last11=Hu |first11=Chiyi |last12=Karam |first12=Elie G. |last13=McGrath |first13=John J. |last14=Navarro-Mateu |first14=Fernando |last15=Scott |first15=Kate M. |date=2025-07-09 |title=Obsessive-compulsive disorder in the World Mental Health surveys |journal=BMC Medicine |language=en |volume=23 |issue=1 |page=416 |doi=10.1186/s12916-025-04209-5 |doi-access=free |pmc=12239380 |pmid=40629326}}</ref> While psychological or pharmacological treatment can lead to a reduction of OCD symptoms and an increase in reported quality of life, symptoms may persist at moderate levels even following adequate treatment courses, and completely symptom-free periods are uncommon.<ref name="pmid15533282">{{cite journal | vauthors = Eddy KT, Dutra L, Bradley R, Westen D | title = A multidimensional meta-analysis of psychotherapy and pharmacotherapy for obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Clinical Psychology Review | volume = 24 | issue = 8 | pages = 1011–1030 | date = December 2004 | pmid = 15533282 | doi = 10.1016/j.cpr.2004.08.004 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Subramaniam M, Soh P, Vaingankar JA, Picco L, Chong SA | title = Quality of life in obsessive-compulsive disorder: impact of the disorder and of treatment | journal = CNS Drugs | volume = 27 | issue = 5 | pages = 367–383 | date = May 2013 | pmid = 23580175 | doi = 10.1007/s40263-013-0056-z }}</ref> In pediatric OCD, around 40% still have the disorder in adulthood and around 40% qualify for [[Remission (medicine)|remission]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Boileau B | title = A review of obsessive-compulsive disorder in children and adolescents | journal = Dialogues in Clinical Neuroscience | volume = 13 | issue = 4 | pages = 401–411 | date = 2011 | pmid = 22275846 | pmc = 3263388 | doi = 10.31887/DCNS.2011.13.4/bboileau }}</ref> The risk of having at least one comorbid personality disorder in OCD is 52%, which is the highest among anxiety disorders and greatly impacts its management and prognosis.<ref name="The role of personality disorders i">{{cite journal | vauthors = Thamby A, Khanna S | title = The role of personality disorders in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Indian Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 61 | issue = Suppl 1 | pages = S114–S118 | date = January 2019 | pmid = 30745684 | pmc = 6343421 | doi = 10.4103/psychiatry.IndianJPsychiatry_526_18 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

== Epidemiology ==
{{Further|Delayed-maturation theory of obsessive–compulsive disorder}}[[File:Obsessive-compulsive disorder world map - DALY - WHO2004.svg|thumb|upright=1.35|Age-standardized [[disability-adjusted life year]] estimated rates for obsessive-compulsive disorder per 100,000 inhabitants in 2004{{Div col|small=yes|colwidth=9em}}
{{legend|#b3b3b3|no data}}
{{legend|#ffff65|<45}}
{{legend|#fff200|45–52.5}}
{{legend|#ffdc00|52.5–60}}
{{legend|#ffc600|60–67.5}}
{{legend|#ffb000|67.5–75}}
{{legend|#ff9a00|75–82.5}}
{{legend|#ff8400|82.5–90}}
{{legend|#ff6e00|90–97.5}}
{{legend|#ff5800|97.5–105}}
{{legend|#ff4200|105–112.5}}
{{legend|#ff2c00|112.5–120}}
{{legend|#cb0000|>120}}
{{div col end}}]]
Obsessive–compulsive disorder affects about 2.3% of people at some point in their life, with the yearly rate about 1.2%.<ref name="Good2014">{{cite journal |vauthors=Goodman WK, Grice DE, Lapidus KA, Coffey BJ |date=September 2014 |title=Obsessive-compulsive disorder |journal=The Psychiatric Clinics of North America |volume=37 |issue=3 |pages=257–267 |doi=10.1016/j.psc.2014.06.004 |pmid=25150561}}</ref> OCD occurs worldwide.<ref name="DSM52" /> It is unusual for symptoms to begin after the age of 35 and half of people develop problems before 20.<ref name="NIH20152" /><ref name="DSM52" /> Over 80% of OCD cases begin by early adulthood.<ref name=":2" /> Males and females are affected about equally.<ref name="NIH20152" /> However, there is an earlier age for onset for males than females.<ref>{{Cite web |date=September 2022 |publisher=National Institute for Mental Health |title=Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD) |url=https://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/topics/obsessive-compulsive-disorder-ocd#:~:text=Obsessive%2Dcompulsive%20disorder%20(OCD),to%20repeat%20over%20and%20over. |access-date=15 March 2023}}</ref>

== History ==
[[Plutarch]], an [[Ancient Greek philosophy|ancient Greek philosopher]] and [[Hellenic historiography|historian]], [[Mental illness in ancient Rome|describes]] an ancient Roman man who possibly had [[scrupulosity]], which could be a symptom of OCD or [[Obsessive–compulsive personality disorder|OCPD]]. This man is described as "turning pale under his [[Wreath (attire)|crown of flowers]]", praying with a "faltering voice" and scattering "incense with trembling hands".<ref>{{Cite book | vauthors = Summers J, Sinnott-Armstrong W |chapter=Obsessive Compulsive Disorder: A Little History |title=Clean Hands: Philosophical Lessons from Scrupulosity |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-19-005869-2 |page=19}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last=Friedrich |first=Patricia |title=The Literary and Linguistic Construction of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder |publisher=Palgrave Macmillan UK |publication-place=London |date=2015 |isbn=978-1-349-57638-8 |doi=10.1057/9781137427335 |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1057/9781137427335 |url-access=subscription |pages=38–39}}</ref><ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Plutarch | title = Selected Lives and Essays | publisher = Classics Club | date = 1951 | page = 375 }}</ref>

In the 7th century AD, [[John Climacus]] records an instance of a young [[monk]] plagued by constant and overwhelming "temptations to [[blasphemy]]" consulting an older monk, who told him: "My son, I take upon myself all the sins which these temptations have led you, or may lead you, to commit. All I require of you is that for the future you pay no attention to them whatsoever."<ref name="Osborn1998">{{Cite book | vauthors = Osborn I |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WVgTAAAAQBAJ&q=Obsessive+Compulsive+Disorder+in+antiquity&pg=PA210 |title=Tormenting Thoughts and Secret Rituals: The Hidden Epidemic of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder |date=1998 |publisher=Dell Publishing |isbn=978-0-440-50847-2 |location=New York City, New York}}</ref>{{rp|212}} ''[[The Cloud of Unknowing]]'', a [[Christians|Christian]] mystical text from the late 14th century, recommends dealing with recurring obsessions by attempting to ignore them, and, if that fails, to "cower under them like a poor wretch and a coward overcome in battle, and reckon it to be a waste of your time for you to strive any longer against them", a technique now known as [[emotional flooding]].<ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|213}}

[[Abu Zayd al-Balkhi]], the 9th-century Islamic polymath, was likely the first to classify OCD into different types and pioneer [[cognitive behavioral therapy]], in a fashion unique to his era and which was not popular in Greek medicine.<ref name="Awaad_2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Awaad R, Ali S | title = Obsessional Disorders in al-Balkhi's 9th century treatise: Sustenance of the Body and Soul | journal = Journal of Affective Disorders | volume = 180 | pages = 185–189 | date = July 2015 | pmid = 25911133 | doi = 10.1016/j.jad.2015.03.003 }}</ref> In his medical treatise entitled ''Sustenance of the Body and Soul'', al-Balkhi described obsessions particular to the disorder as "Annoying thoughts that are not real. These intrusive thoughts prevent enjoying life, and performing daily activities. They affect concentration and interfere with ability to carry out different tasks."<ref>{{Cite book | vauthors = Al-Balkhi A, Misri M, al-Hayyat M |title=Masālih al-Abdān Wa-al-Anfus |publisher=Ma'had al-Makhtūtāt al-'Arabīyah |year=2005 |location=Cairo |language=ar}}</ref> As treatment, al-Balkhi suggested treating obsessive thoughts with positive thoughts and mind-based therapy.<ref name="Awaad_2015" />

From the 14th to the 16th century in Europe, it was believed that people who experienced blasphemous, sexual or other obsessive thoughts were [[Spiritual possession|possessed]] by the [[Satan|devil]].<ref name="Aardema, F. 2007">{{Cite journal | vauthors = Aardema F, O'Connor K |year=2007 |title=The menace within: obsessions and the self |journal=Journal of Cognitive Psychotherapy |volume=21 |issue=3 |pages=182–197 |doi=10.1891/088983907781494573 }}</ref><ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|213}} Based on this reasoning, treatment involved banishing the "evil" from the "possessed" person through [[exorcism]].<ref name="Jenike">{{cite book | vauthors = Jenike MA, Baer L, Minichiello WE | title = Obsessive Compulsive Disorders: Theory and Management | location = Littleton, MA | publisher = PSG Publishing | date = 1986 }}</ref><ref name="berrios">{{cite journal | vauthors = Berrios GE | title = Obsessive-compulsive disorder: its conceptual history in France during the 19th century | journal = Comprehensive Psychiatry | volume = 30 | issue = 4 | pages = 283–295 | year = 1989 | pmid = 2667880 | doi = 10.1016/0010-440x(89)90052-7 }}</ref> The vast majority of people who thought that they were possessed by the devil did not have hallucinations or other "spectacular symptoms" but "complained of anxiety, religious fears, and evil thoughts".<ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|213}} In 1584, a woman from [[Kent]], [[England]], named Mrs. Davie, described by a [[justice of the peace]] as "a good wife", was nearly [[Death by burning|burned at the stake]] after she confessed that she experienced constant, unwanted urges to murder her family.<ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|213}}

The English term ''obsessive–compulsive'' arose as a translation of [[German language|German]] {{Lang|de|Zwangsvorstellung}} (''obsession'') used in the first conceptions of OCD by [[Karl Friedrich Otto Westphal|Karl Westphal]]. Westphal's description went on to influence [[Pierre Janet]], who further documented features of OCD.<ref name="Sadock" /> In the early 1910s, [[Sigmund Freud]] attributed obsessive–compulsive behavior to unconscious conflicts that manifest as symptoms.<ref name="Jenike" /> Freud describes the clinical history of a typical case of "touching phobia" as starting in early childhood, when the person has a strong desire to touch an item. In response, the person develops an "external prohibition" against this type of touching. However, this "prohibition does not succeed in abolishing" the desire to touch; all it can do is repress the desire and "force it into the unconscious".<ref>{{Cite book | vauthors = Freud S |title=Totem and Taboo:Some Points of Agreement between the Mental Lives of Savages and Neurotics |title-link=Totem and Taboo:Some Points of Agreement between the Mental Lives of Savages and Neurotics |publisher=W. W. Norton & Company |translator-last1 = Strachey |translator-first1 = J |year=1950 |isbn=978-0-393-00143-3 |location=New York |author-link=Sigmund Freud | page = 29 }}</ref> Freudian [[psychoanalysis]] remained the dominant treatment for OCD until the mid-1980s, even though medicinal and therapeutic treatments were known and available, because it was widely thought that these treatments would be detrimental to the effectiveness of the [[psychotherapy]].<ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|210–211}} In the mid-1980s, this approach changed and practitioners began treating OCD primarily with medicine and practical therapy rather than through psychoanalysis.<ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|210}}

One of the first successful treatments of OCD, [[Exposure therapy|exposure and response prevention]], emerged during the 1960s, when psychologist [[Vic Meyer]] exposed two hospitalized patients to anxiety-inducing situations while preventing them from performing any compulsions. Eventually, both patients' anxiety level dropped to manageable levels. Meyer devised this procedure from his analysis of [[Extinction (psychology)|fear extinguishment]] in animals via [[Flooding (psychology)|flooding]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Meyer V | title = Modification of expectations in cases with obsessional rituals | journal = Behaviour Research and Therapy | volume = 4 | issue = 4 | pages = 273–280 | date = November 1966 | pmid = 5978682 | doi = 10.1016/0005-7967(66)90023-4 }}</ref> The success of ERP clinically and scientifically has been summarized as "spectacular" by prominent OCD researcher [[Stanley Rachman]] decades following Meyer's creation of the method.<ref name="Behavior Therapy for OCD">{{Cite web|url=http://www.ocdhistory.net/20thcentury/behaviortherapy.html|title=The history of obsessive-compulsive disorder|website=www.ocdhistory.net}}</ref>

In 1967, psychiatrist [[Juan José López-Ibor]] reported that the drug [[clomipramine]] was effective in treating OCD. Many reports of its success in treatment followed and several studies had confirmed its effectiveness by the 1980s.<ref>{{Cite book | vauthors = Osborn I |title=Tormenting Thoughts and Secret Rituals: The Hidden Epidemic of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder |publisher=[[Dell Publishing]] |year=1999 |isbn=978-0-440-50847-2 |location=New York}}</ref><ref name="ocdhistory.net">{{Cite web |title=The history of obsessive-compulsive disorder |url=https://ocdhistory.net/20thcentury/new_meds.html |access-date=4 November 2023 |website=ocdhistory.net}}</ref> However, clomipramine was subsequently displaced by new [[Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor|SSRIs]] developed in the 1970s, such as [[fluoxetine]] and [[sertraline]], which were shown to have fewer side effects.<ref name="NEJM2014" /><ref name="ocdhistory.net"/>

Many people, particularly those with contamination-related OCD, experienced new or worsened symptoms related to [[COVID-19]] during the first few months of [[COVID-19 pandemic|the pandemic]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Guzick AG, Candelari A, Wiese AD, Schneider SC, Goodman WK, Storch EA | title = Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder During the COVID-19 Pandemic: a Systematic Review | journal = Current Psychiatry Reports | volume = 23 | issue = 11 | article-number = 71 | date = October 2021 | pmid = 34613498 | pmc = 8493778 | doi = 10.1007/s11920-021-01284-2 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

=== Notable cases ===
[[John Bunyan]] (1628–1688), the author of ''[[The Pilgrim's Progress]]'', displayed symptoms of OCD (which had not yet been named). During the most severe period of his condition, he would mutter the same phrase over and over again to himself while rocking back and forth.<ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|53–54}} He later described his obsessions in his autobiography ''[[Grace Abounding to the Chief of Sinners]]'', stating, "These things may seem ridiculous to others, even as ridiculous as they were in themselves, but to me they were the most tormenting cogitations."<ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|53–54}} He wrote two pamphlets advising those with similar anxieties.<ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|217–218}} In one of them, he warns against indulging in compulsions: "Have care of putting off your trouble of spirit in the wrong way: by promising to reform yourself and lead a new life, by your performances or duties."<ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|217–218}}

British poet, essayist and [[Lexicography|lexicographer]] [[Samuel Johnson]] (1709–1784) had OCD. He had elaborate rituals for crossing the thresholds of doorways and repeatedly walked up and down staircases counting the steps.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Samuel Johnson (1709–1784): A Patron Saint of OCD? | vauthors = Penzel F | work = International OCD Foundation |url=http://westsuffolkpsych.homestead.com/Johnson.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20050618085836/http://westsuffolkpsych.homestead.com/Johnson.html |archive-date= 18 June 2005 | access-date=29 November 2013 | via = Westsuffolkpsych.homestead.com}}</ref><ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|54–55}} He would touch every post on the street as he walked past, only step in the middle of paving stones and repeatedly perform tasks as though they had not been done properly the first time.<ref name="Osborn1998" />{{rp|55}}

The "[[Rat Man]]", real name Ernst Lanzer, a patient of [[Sigmund Freud]], suffered from what was then called "obsessional neurosis". Lanzer's illness was characterised most famously by a pattern of distressing intrusive thoughts in which he feared that his father or a female friend would be subjected to a purported Chinese method of [[torture]] in which rats would be encouraged to gnaw their way out of a victim's body by a hot poker.<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Waude A |title=Rat Man Case Study: Sigmund Freud's Search For The Cause Of One Man's Obsessive Thoughts. |url=https://www.psychologistworld.com/freud/rat-man-case-study |website=www.psychologistworld.com |language=en |date=29 March 2016}}</ref>
