American aviator and filmmaker [[Howard Hughes]] is known to have had OCD, primarily an obsessive fear of germs and contamination.<ref>{{Cite web | vauthors = Dittmann M |date=July–August 2005 |title=Hughes's germ phobia revealed in psychological autopsy |url=http://www.apa.org/monitor/julaug05/hughes.aspx |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150105071029/http://www.apa.org/monitor/julaug05/hughes.aspx |archive-date=5 January 2015 |access-date=9 January 2015 |website=American Psychological Association}}</ref> Friends of Hughes have also mentioned his obsession with minor flaws in clothing.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors =  Dittmann M |date=July–August 2005 |title=Hughes's germ phobia revealed in psychological autopsy |url=http://www.apa.org/monitor/julaug05/hughes.html |url-status=live |journal=Monitor on Psychology |volume=36 |issue=7 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081229051558/http://www.apa.org/monitor/julaug05/hughes.html |archive-date=29 December 2008}}</ref> This was conveyed in ''[[The Aviator (2004 film)|The Aviator]]'' (2004), a film biography of Hughes.<ref name="MGHOCD">{{Cite web | vauthors = Chosak A |date=12 October 2012 |title=The Aviator: A real-life portrayal of OCD in the media |url=https://mghocd.org/the-aviator-ocd-in-the-media/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150222164535/https://mghocd.org/the-aviator-ocd-in-the-media/ |archive-date=22 February 2015 |access-date=9 January 2015 |website=Massachusetts General Hospital OCD and Related Disorders Program}}</ref>

English singer-songwriter [[George Ezra]] has spoken about his life-long struggle with OCD, particularly [[primarily obsessional obsessive–compulsive disorder]].<ref>{{Cite news |date=31 August 2020 |title=George Ezra opens up about OCD struggle |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/entertainment-arts-53950320#:~:text=George%20Ezra%20says%20he%20is,physical%20actions%20to%20relieve%20them.&text=Discovering%20the%20condition%20existed%20made,less%20alone%2C%22%20he%20added.}}</ref>

Swedish climate activist [[Greta Thunberg]] is also known to have OCD, among other mental health conditions.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 February 2020 |title=Greta Thunberg was nearly hospitalised due to disordered eating, says mother |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/life-style/health-and-families/greta-thunberg-aspergers-eating-disorder-malena-ernman-interview-a9355201.html |website=[[Independent.co.uk]]}}</ref>

''Passing for Normal: A Memoir of Compulsion'' (2000) is a [[memoir]] by [[Amy Wilensky]] about her experience of OCD and [[Tourette syndrome]].<ref>{{cite journal | last=Geller | first=Jeffrey L. | title=Twitch and Shout; Passing for Normal: A Memoir of Compulsion; Motherless Brooklyn | journal=Psychiatric Services | volume=51 | issue=11 | date=2000 | doi=10.1176/appi.ps.51.11.1455-a | pages=1455–a–1457 }}</ref>

American actor [[James Spader]] has spoken about his OCD. In 2014, when interviewed for ''[[Rolling Stone]]'' he said: "I'm obsessive-compulsive. I have very, very strong obsessive-compulsive issues. I'm very particular. ... It's very hard for me, you know? It makes you very addictive in behavior, because routine and ritual become entrenched. But in work, it manifests itself in obsessive attention to detail and fixation. It serves my work very well: Things don't slip by. But I'm not very easygoing."<ref>{{Cite magazine | vauthors = Goldman A |date=21 April 2014 |title=James Spader: The Strangest Man on TV |url=https://www.rollingstone.com/tv/tv-news/james-spader-the-strangest-man-on-tv-173892/ |magazine=[[Rolling Stone]] |access-date=10 July 2023}}</ref>

In 2022 the president of Chile [[Gabriel Boric]] stated that he had OCD, saying: "I have an obsessive–compulsive disorder that's completely under control. Thank God I've been able to undergo treatment and it doesn't make me unable to carry out my responsibilities as the President of the Republic."<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lXNsCwFHNH8 |title=EXCLUSIVE: Chile's President On Why He's Open About His OCD |publisher=Amanpour and Company |date=26 September 2022 |via=[[YouTube]] |access-date=10 July 2023}}</ref>

In a documentary released in 2023, the footballer [[David Beckham]] discussed his compelling cleaning rituals, need for symmetry in the fridge and the impact of OCD on his life.<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Banfield-Nwachi M |title=David Beckham reveals impact of OCD in new documentary |url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2023/apr/28/david-beckham-ocd-obsessive-compulsive-disorder-netflix-documentary |website=The Guardian |date=28 April 2023}}</ref>

In 2018, American rapper [[NF (rapper)|NF]] was diagnosed with obsessive–compulsive disorder.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lavin |first=Will |date=2019-09-11 |title=Rapper NF: "I was having a mental breakdown. I was questioning my life. I was questioning everything" |url=https://www.nme.com/features/music-interviews/nf-i-was-having-a-mental-breakdown-i-was-questioning-my-life-i-was-questioning-everything-2546348 |access-date=2025-12-24 |website=NME |language=en-GB}}</ref> He addresses his experience with mental illness and therapy frequently in his music, particularly in the 2023 album [[Hope (NF album)|''Hope'']].

In 2025, actress [[Jenna Ortega]] revealed that she struggles with it noting: "(I have) pretty intense OCD. Repetitive thoughts and counting everything multiple times and having to do the same action over and over... Sometimes my nights consist of just being really exhausted and going up and down the stairs six times because that's what I think I need to do to make sure no one breaks into my home."<ref>{{cite web |author1=Health Desk |title=''Wednesday'' Actor Jenna Ortega Opens Up About Living with OCD: 'I Go Up and Down the Stairs Six Times' |url=https://www.financialexpress.com/life/lifestyle-wednesday-actor-jenna-ortega-opens-up-about-living-with-ocd-i-go-up-and-down-the-stairs-six-times-3941126/ |website=[[The Financial Express (India)|The Financial Express]] |access-date=September 14, 2025 |language=en |date=August 9, 2025}}</ref><ref name="Anbouba">{{cite web |last1=Anbouba |first1=Margaux |title=24 Goth Hours with Jenna Ortega |url=https://www.vogue.com/article/24-goth-hours-with-jenna-ortega |website=[[Vogue (magazine)|Vogue]] |access-date=September 14, 2025 |date=August 25, 2025}}</ref>

== Society and culture ==
===Art, entertainment and media===
Movies and television shows may portray idealized or incomplete representations of disorders such as OCD.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Fennell D, Boyd M |date=2 September 2014 |title=Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder in the Media |journal=Deviant Behavior |volume=35 |issue=9 |pages=669–686 |doi=10.1080/01639625.2013.872526 }}</ref> Compassionate and accurate literary and on-screen depictions may help counteract the potential [[Social stigma against mental disorders|stigma]] associated with an OCD diagnosis and lead to increased public awareness, understanding and sympathy for such disorders.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Don't Be Afraid of the Word "Disorder" |url=https://beyondocd.org/expert-perspectives/articles/dont-be-afraid-of-the-word-disorder |access-date=11 November 2020 |website=Beyond OCD |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="Goldberg">{{Cite book | vauthors = Goldberg FR |url=http://secure.ce4alliance.com/articles/101188/Turn_Box_Office_Movies-CE%5B1%5D.pdf |title=Turn box office movies into mental health opportunities: A literature review and resource guide for clinicians and educators |date=2007 |publisher=Beneficial Film Guides, Inc. |page=8 |access-date=17 February 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110708130111/http://secure.ce4alliance.com/articles/101188/Turn_Box_Office_Movies-CE%5B1%5D.pdf |archive-date=8 July 2011 }}</ref>
* The play and film adaptations of'' [[The Odd Couple (film)|The Odd Couple]]'' based around the character of Felix, who shows some of the common symptoms of OCD.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Felix Unger from The Odd Couple|website=CharacTour|url=https://www.charactour.com/hub/characters/view/Felix-Unger.The-Odd-Couple}}</ref>
* In the film ''[[As Good as It Gets]]'' (1997), actor [[Jack Nicholson]] portrays a man with OCD who performs ritualistic behaviors that disrupt his life.<ref name="mghocd.org">{{Cite web | vauthors = Berman N |date=5 October 2012 |title=Is This 'As Good as It Gets?': Popular Media's Representation of OCD |url= https://mghocd.org/as-good-as-it-gets/ |url-status=live |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20150222163353/https://mghocd.org/as-good-as-it-gets/ |archive-date=22 February 2015 |access-date=9 January 2015 |website=Massachusetts General Hospital OCD and Related Disorders Program}}</ref>
* The film ''[[Matchstick Men]]'' (2003) portrays a con man named Roy ([[Nicolas Cage]]) with OCD who opens and closes doors three times while counting aloud before he can walk through them.<ref>{{Cite web | vauthors = Almeida J |title=Royal College of Psychiatrists, Discover Psychiatry, Minds on Film Blog, Matchstick Men |url=http://www.rcpsych.ac.uk/mentalhealthinfo/mindsonfilmblog/matchstickmen.aspx |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150222173938/http://www.rcpsych.ac.uk/mentalhealthinfo/mindsonfilmblog/matchstickmen.aspx |archive-date=22 February 2015 |access-date=14 January 2015 |website=Royal College of Psychiatrists}}</ref>
* [[Martin Scorsese]]'s biographical drama film ''[[The Aviator (2004 film)|The Aviator]]'' (2004) portrays the life of [[Howard Hughes]] from 1927 to 1947, when he became a successful film producer and an aviation magnate while simultaneously growing more unstable due to severe obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD).<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Aviator {{!}} Reviews {{!}} guardian.co.uk Film |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/News_Story/Critic_Review/Guardian_Film_of_the_week/0,4267,1379282,00.html |access-date=2026-04-28 |website=[[The Guardian]] |quote=Howard Hughes - businessman, aeronautics maestro, Hollywood producer and of course fearless pilot - is arguably history's greatest undiagnosed sufferer from obsessive-compulsive disorder.}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Winter |first=Jessica |date=2005-06-10 |title=The Aviator |url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2005/jun/10/dvdreviews |access-date=2026-04-28 |work=[[The Guardian]] |language=en-GB |issn=0261-3077 |quote=Jaunty, propulsive and a visual feast, The Aviator spans 20-odd years in the life of Howard Hughes (Leonardo DiCaprio): drill-bit heir, Hollywood arriviste, serial ladykiller, cash-haemorrhaging businessman, germophobe OCD sufferer, and aviation pioneer.}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2008-09-26 |title=Leonardo DiCaprio developed Howard Hughes syndrome |url=https://forum.psychlinks.ca/threads/leonardo-dicaprio-developed-howard-hughes-syndrome.10519/ |access-date=2026-04-28 |website=Psychlinks Forum -- Archive Only (2004-2022) |language=en-US}}</ref>
* In the television series ''[[Monk (TV series)|Monk]]'' (2002–2009), the titular character [[Adrian Monk]] fears both human contact and dirt.<ref>{{Cite news | vauthors = Stewart S |date=16 September 2007 |title=Happy to Be Neurotic, at Least Once a Week |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2007/09/16/arts/television/16stew.html |url-status=live |access-date=8 December 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081211174041/http://www.nytimes.com/2007/09/16/arts/television/16stew.html |archive-date=11 December 2008}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Anxiety Disorders Association of America |title=What Is OCD? |url=http://www.usanetwork.com/series/monk/community/ocd/index.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081217130053/http://www.usanetwork.com/series/monk/community/ocd/index.html |archive-date=17 December 2008 |access-date=8 December 2008 |website=USA Network}}</ref>
* The one-man show ''[[The Life and Slimes of Marc Summers]]'' (2016), a stage adaptation of [[Marc Summers]]' 1999 memoir which recounts how OCD affected his entertainment career.<ref name="Gioia 2016 n498">{{cite web | vauthors = Gioia M | title=Double Dare Host Marc Summers Crossed Paths With Broadway, and Now He's Got a Solo Show | website=Playbill | date=6 April 2016 | url=https://playbill.com/article/double-dare-host-marc-summers-crossed-paths-with-broadway-and-now-hes-got-a-solo-show | access-date=27 February 2024}}</ref>
* In the novel ''[[Turtles All the Way Down (novel)|Turtles All the Way Down]]'' (2017) by [[John Green]], teenage main character Aza Holmes struggles with OCD that manifests as a fear of the human microbiome. Throughout the story, Aza repeatedly opens an unhealed [[callus]] on her finger to drain out what she believes are pathogens. The novel is based on Green's own experiences with OCD. He explained that ''Turtles All the Way Down'' is intended to show how "most people with chronic mental illnesses also live long, fulfilling lives."<ref>{{Cite news | vauthors = Flood A |date=14 October 2017 |title=John Green: 'Having OCD is an ongoing part of my life' |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/books/2017/oct/14/john-green-turtles-all-the-way-down-ocd-interview |access-date=21 September 2019}}</ref>
* In the film [[The House That Jack Built (2018 film)|''The House That Jack Built'']] (2018), the titular character, a [[serial killer]], compulsively cleans a crime scene after obsessing over leaving evidence.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Smith |first=Carter Trey |date=August 22, 2022 |title=OCD Representation in 'The House that Jack Built' and Associated Media |url=https://www.incluvie.com/articles/ocd-representation-in-the-house-that-jack-built-and-associated-media |access-date=27 May 2025 |website=Incluvie |language=en}}</ref>
* The British TV series ''[[Pure (British TV series)|Pure]]'' (2019) stars [[Charly Clive]] as 24-year-old Marnie, who is plagued by disturbing sexual thoughts, as a kind of [[primarily obsessional obsessive compulsive disorder]].<ref>{{Cite news | vauthors = Mangan L |date=30 January 2019 |title=Pure review – a masterly comedy about sex and mental health |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/tv-and-radio/2019/jan/30/pure-o-ocd-review-brave-brilliant-miracle-mental-health-sex |access-date=7 April 2021}}</ref>

== Research ==
{{See also|List of investigational obsessive–compulsive disorder drugs}}

[[Cerebral cortex|Cortical thickness]] differences in adults with OCD are linked to a network of brain-specific, developmentally expressed genes.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Zhang D, Teng C, Xu Y, Tian L, Cao P, Wang X, Li Z, Guan C, Hu X | title = Genetic and molecular correlates of cortical thickness alterations in adults with obsessive-compulsive disorder: a transcription-neuroimaging association analysis | journal = Psychological Medicine | volume = 54 | issue = 12 | pages = 3469–3478 | date = October 2024 | pmid = 39363543 | pmc = 11496223 | doi = 10.1017/s0033291724001909 }}</ref>

OCD patients have thicker precentral and paracentral brain regions compared to controls and those with thinner precentral areas showed greater improvement with [[transcranial direct-current stimulation]] treatment, suggesting these structural differences may serve as neural biomarkers for predicting treatment response.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Harika-Germaneau G, Gosez J, Bokam P, Guillevin R, Doolub D, Thirioux B, Wassouf I, Germaneau A, Langbour N, Jaafari N | title = Investigating brain structure and tDCS response in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Journal of Psychiatric Research | volume = 177 | pages = 39–45 | date = September 2024 | pmid = 38971055 | doi = 10.1016/j.jpsychires.2024.06.053 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

[[Glutamatergic]] medications, such as [[Acetylcysteine|N-acetylcysteine]] (NAC) and [[memantine]], may improve symptoms of OCD, though high [[Homogeneity and heterogeneity|heterogeneity]] and potential [[publication bias]] warrant cautious interpretation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Coelho |first1=David R. A. |last2=Yang |first2=Chen |last3=Suriaga |first3=Armiel |last4=Manasa |first4=Justen |last5=Bain |first5=Paul A. |last6=Vieira |first6=Willians Fernando |last7=Papatheodorou |first7=Stefania |last8=Salvi |first8=Joshua D. |date=2025-01-02 |title=Glutamatergic Medications for Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis |journal=JAMA Network Open |volume=8 |issue=1 |pages=e2452963 |doi=10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2024.52963 |pmc=11696454 |pmid=39745698}}</ref> Augmenting SSRIs with NAC may modestly reduce OCD symptoms without increasing adverse events,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Eghdami |first1=Shayan |last2=Eissazade |first2=Negin |last3=Heidari Mokarar |first3=Mohsen |last4=Boroon |first4=Mahsa |last5=Orsolini |first5=Laura |last6=Shalbafan |first6=Mohammadreza |date=2024 |title=The safety and efficacy of N-acetylcysteine as an augmentation in the treatment of obsessive-compulsive disorder in adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials |journal=Frontiers in Psychiatry |volume=15 |article-number=1421150 |doi=10.3389/fpsyt.2024.1421150 |doi-access=free |pmc=11456833 |pmid=39376972}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gadallah |first1=Al-Hussein Ahmed |last2=Ebada |first2=Mahmoud Ahmed |last3=Gadallah |first3=Ahmed |last4=Ahmed |first4=Hussien |last5=Rashad |first5=Wegdan |last6=Eid |first6=Khalid Adel |last7=Bahbah |first7=Eshak |last8=Alkanj |first8=Souad |date=2020-04-01 |title=Efficacy and safety of N-acetylcysteine as add-on therapy in the treatment of obsessive-compulsive disorder: A systematic review and meta-analysis |journal=Journal of Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders |volume=25 |article-number=100529 |doi=10.1016/j.jocrd.2020.100529 }}</ref> though it may have limited efficacy.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Noskova |first1=Eliska |last2=Stopkova |first2=Pavla |last3=Horacek |first3=Jiri |last4=Sebela |first4=Antonin |title=Augmentation therapy of N-acetylcysteine for OCD: A meta-analysis of double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trials |journal=Journal of Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders |date=October 2019 |volume=23 |article-number=100481 |doi=10.1016/j.jocrd.2019.100481 }}</ref> [[Ketamine]], an [[N-Methyl-D-aspartic acid|N-methyl-D-aspartate]] (NMDA) glutamate receptor antagonist, has shown potential for rapid and tolerable symptom relief in OCD, but evidence is limited and inconsistent.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bandeira |first1=Igor D. |last2=Lins-Silva |first2=Daniel H. |last3=Cavenaghi |first3=Vitor Breseghello |last4=Dorea-Bandeira |first4=Ingrid |last5=Faria-Guimarães |first5=Daniela |last6=Barouh |first6=Judah L. |last7=Jesus-Nunes |first7=Ana Paula |last8=Beanes |first8=Graziele |last9=Souza |first9=Lucca S. |last10=Leal |first10=Gustavo C. |last11=Sanacora |first11=Gerard |last12=Miguel |first12=Euripedes C. |last13=Sampaio |first13=Aline S. |last14=Quarantini |first14=Lucas C. |date=2022 |title=Ketamine in the Treatment of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Systematic Review |journal=Harvard Review of Psychiatry |volume=30 |issue=2 |pages=135–145 |doi=10.1097/HRP.0000000000000330 |pmid=35267254}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kwan |first1=Angela T. H. |last2=Lakhani |first2=Moiz |last3=Singh |first3=Gurkaran |last4=Le |first4=Gia Han |last5=Wong |first5=Sabrina |last6=Teopiz |first6=Kayla M. |last7=Dev |first7=Donovan A. |last8=Manku |first8=Arshpreet Singh |last9=Sidhu |first9=Gurnoor |last10=McIntyre |first10=Roger S. |date=2024-11-20 |title=Ketamine for the Treatment of Psychiatric Disorders: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis |journal=CNS Spectrums |volume=29 |issue=5 |pages=354–361 |doi=10.1017/S1092852924000580 |pmid=39564613}}</ref>

[[Psychedelic drug|Psychedelics]] have been investigated for their potential use in treating OCD; [[psilocybin]] is generally well-tolerated in OCD, with symptom reduction for some patients, but repeated dosing may be needed to maintain effects.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Graziosi |first1=Marianna |last2=Rohde |first2=Julia S. |last3=Tiwari |first3=Praachi |last4=Siev |first4=Jedidiah |last5=Yaden |first5=David B. |title=Psychedelics, OCD and related disorders: A systematic review |journal=Journal of Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders |date=April 2024 |volume=41 |article-number=100873 |doi=10.1016/j.jocrd.2024.100873 }}</ref>
